stem: Classic presentation of GBS?

choices.text: Ascending symmetric weakness with areflexia following a recent infection
choices.feedback: Correct! Classic GBS: 1-4 weeks post-infection → ascending symmetric weakness (legs → arms → respiratory → cranial nerves) + areflexia/hyporeflexia (LMN). Campylobacter jejuni is the #1 infectious trigger.

choices.text: Descending paralysis starting with cranial nerves
choices.feedback: Descending = botulism or Miller Fisher variant, not classic GBS.

choices.text: Fluctuating weakness worse at end of day
choices.feedback: Fatigable weakness = myasthenia gravis, not GBS.

choices.text: Unilateral facial droop with forehead sparing
choices.feedback: Upper motor neuron facial palsy (stroke). GBS can cause bilateral facial weakness.

explanation: GBS classically presents with ascending symmetric weakness beginning in the distal lower extremities and progressing proximally (legs→trunk→arms→face/respiratory muscles), with areflexia/hyporeflexia, typically 1-4 weeks after a preceding infection (upper respiratory or gastrointestinal). Sensory symptoms may occur but motor weakness predominates. Autonomic dysfunction (tachycardia, BP fluctuations, urinary retention) occurs in ~70%. The weakness is symmetric, distinguishing it from focal neurologic conditions.

stem: CSF findings in GBS?

choices.feedback: Low glucose + high WBC = bacterial meningitis.
Elevated WBC suggests infection (meningitis), not GBS.

Correct! Classic CSF finding in GBS. Elevated protein from inflammation-related breakdown of blood-nerve barrier. Normal cell count distinguishes from infectious processes. May be normal in the first week — repeat LP if suspicion remains high.
While possible early, elevated protein with normal cells is the classic finding.

explanation: Albuminocytologic dissociation — elevated CSF protein with normal cell count — is the hallmark CSF finding in GBS. Protein elevation reflects blood-nerve barrier disruption and demyelination. Cell count remains normal because GBS is not an infectious or inflammatory meningeal process. Note that CSF may be normal in the first week; the classic finding appears after 1-2 weeks. Elevated WBC in CSF should prompt consideration of alternative diagnoses (HIV polyradiculopathy, Lyme disease, CMV).

stem: When should you intubate a GBS patient?

choices.text: Only when the patient becomes unresponsive
choices.feedback: Waiting for unresponsiveness risks respiratory arrest.

choices.text: FVC <20 mL/kg or NIF < -30 cmH2O (20/30 rule)
choices.feedback: Correct! Monitor pulmonary function serially. Intubate when FVC <target rate or NIF (negative inspiratory force) weaker than -30 cmH2O. Also intubate for: rapid decline in FVC (>30% drop), bulbar dysfunction with aspiration risk, inability to cough effectively. 30% of GBS patients need ventilation.

choices.text: Based on arterial blood gas showing CO2 retention
choices.feedback: CO2 retention is a very late finding. FVC/NIF monitoring catches decline earlier.
SpO2 drops LATE in neuromuscular respiratory failure — waiting for desaturation is too late.

explanation: The 20/30 rule for intubation in GBS: intubate when forced vital capacity (FVC) drops below 20 mL/kg or negative inspiratory force (NIF) is weaker than -30 cmH2O. These thresholds indicate respiratory muscle weakness insufficient to maintain adequate ventilation. Approximately 30% of GBS patients require mechanical ventilation. Serial bedside pulmonary function monitoring (FVC, NIF) every 4-6 hours is essential. Do not rely solely on oxygen saturation — it may remain normal until respiratory arrest is imminent.

stem: Which treatment is NOT effective in GBS?

choices.feedback: IVIG is first-line treatment for GBS.
Supportive care is essential for GBS management.
Plasmapheresis is equally effective as IVIG for GBS.

Correct! HIGH-YIELD: Steroids are NOT effective in GBS (unlike most other autoimmune conditions). Multiple trials show no benefit. IVIG + steroids = no additional benefit. Do NOT give steroids for GBS. This distinguishes GBS from CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), where steroids ARE effective.

explanation: Corticosteroids are NOT effective in GBS and may even worsen outcomes — multiple randomized trials have shown no benefit. This distinguishes GBS from CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), which does respond to steroids. First-line GBS treatments are IVIG (0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days) or plasmapheresis (5 exchanges over 1-2 weeks), both equally effective. Treatment should begin within 2 weeks of symptom onset for maximum benefit.

stem: Miller Fisher variant of GBS presents with:

choices.feedback: Systemic inflammatory signs suggest other autoimmune conditions, not GBS.

Correct! Miller Fisher triad: ophthalmoplegia (eye movement paralysis) + ataxia + areflexia. Associated with anti-GQ1b antibodies. Descending pattern (starts cranially). Generally better prognosis than classic AIDP. Treat with IVIG or plasmapheresis.
CNS symptoms suggest encephalitis, not GBS variants.
That's classic GBS. Miller Fisher is different.

explanation: Miller Fisher syndrome (MFS) is a GBS variant characterized by the triad of ophthalmoplegia (eye movement paralysis), ataxia (cerebellar-type gait unsteadiness), and areflexia. It is strongly associated with anti-GQ1b antibodies (~85% positive). MFS accounts for ~5% of GBS cases and generally has an excellent prognosis. Unlike typical GBS, limb weakness is usually absent or mild. Anti-GQ1b antibodies target gangliosides concentrated in cranial nerves III, IV, VI and cerebellar neurons.

stem: #1 infectious trigger for GBS?

choices.feedback: Influenza can trigger GBS but is not the #1 cause.

Correct! C. jejuni gastroenteritis precedes ~30% of GBS cases. The lipopolysaccharides on C. jejuni resemble gangliosides on peripheral nerves → molecular mimicry → autoimmune attack. C. jejuni-associated GBS tends to have worse prognosis (often axonal variant — AMAN).
E. coli is a common GI pathogen but not a major GBS trigger.
S. aureus is not typically associated with GBS.

explanation: Campylobacter jejuni gastroenteritis is the most common infectious trigger for GBS, preceding approximately 25-40% of cases. The mechanism involves molecular mimicry between Campylobacter lipooligosaccharides and gangliosides (GM1, GD1a, GQ1b) on peripheral nerve myelin/axons, triggering autoimmune cross-reactivity. Other triggers include CMV, EBV, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Zika virus, and influenza. GBS can also occur post-vaccination, though the absolute risk is extremely low.

stem: GBS vs botulism — key distinguishing feature?

choices.feedback: GBS ascends; botulism DESCENDS. This is the key distinction.

choices.text: GBS: ascending weakness. Botulism: descending weakness starting with cranial nerves
choices.feedback: Correct! GBS = ascending (legs → arms → respiratory). Botulism = descending (cranial nerves → arms → legs). Botulism also has: dilated/fixed pupils, autonomic dysfunction, associated with home-canned foods (C. botulinum toxin blocks ACh release). Both cause flaccid paralysis and areflexia.

choices.text: GBS has UMN signs; botulism has LMN signs
choices.feedback: Both are LMN patterns with flaccid weakness and areflexia.
Both GBS and botulism can have autonomic dysfunction.

explanation: The key distinguishing feature between GBS and botulism is the direction of weakness: GBS produces ascending weakness (legs→arms→face/respiratory), while botulism produces descending weakness (cranial nerves first: diplopia, dysphagia, dysarthria→limbs→respiratory). Both can cause respiratory failure. Botulism also features dilated/fixed pupils and autonomic dysfunction from cholinergic blockade. Botulism is caused by Clostridium botulinum toxin blocking acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction.

stem: Most common type of primary headache?

choices.feedback: Correct! Tension-type is the most common primary headache (~70% of primary headaches). Bilateral, band-like pressure, mild-moderate severity. No aura, typically no N/V or photophobia. Associated with pericranial muscle tenderness and stress.
TN is relatively uncommon and classified differently.
Cluster is the least common of the three major primary headaches.
Migraine is the second most common. Tension-type is more prevalent.

explanation: Tension-type headache is the most prevalent primary headache disorder, affecting ~40% of the population. It presents with bilateral, non-pulsating, band-like pressure of mild-to-moderate intensity without nausea or significant sensory sensitivity. First-line treatment is simple analgesics (NSAIDs, acetaminophen). Preventive therapy with amitriptyline is considered for chronic TTH (≥15 days/month).

stem: Behavioral difference between migraine and cluster headache?

choices.feedback: Correct! Migraine → photophobia, worsened by movement, patients lie in dark quiet room. Cluster → severe pain causes restlessness — patients pace, rock, bang head. This behavioral difference is a high-yield board pearl for distinguishing the two.

choices.text: Cluster patients prefer standing; migraine patients prefer sitting
choices.feedback: It's more about agitation (cluster) vs stillness (migraine).
The behavioral distinction is one of the most reliable clinical clues.

choices.text: Both patients lie still in dark rooms
choices.feedback: Only migraine patients prefer stillness. Cluster patients are agitated.

explanation: A key behavioral distinction: migraine patients prefer to lie still in a dark, quiet room (movement worsens pain), while cluster headache patients are characteristically restless and agitated, often pacing, rocking, or even banging their head against a wall during attacks. This behavioral difference is diagnostically useful. Both involve severe pain, but cluster headache produces more extreme agitation due to the excruciating unilateral periorbital pain and autonomic activation.

stem: First-line acute treatment for cluster headache?

choices.feedback: Simple analgesics are ineffective for the severe pain of cluster headache.

Correct! 100% O2 at target rate via non-rebreather × 15-20 min is first-line. ~70% response rate within 15 min. Also effective: subcutaneous sumatriptan (fastest triptan route). Prophylaxis: verapamil (first-line). Prednisone for cluster period bridge.
Opioids are ineffective for cluster headache and should be avoided.
Oral triptans are too slow for cluster headache (peaks in 15-180 min). SC sumatriptan or high-flow O2 needed.

explanation: High-flow 100% oxygen (12-15 L/min via non-rebreather mask for 15-20 minutes) is first-line acute treatment for cluster headache, providing rapid relief in 70-80% of attacks. Subcutaneous sumatriptan is equally effective. Inhaled oxygen works through uncertain mechanisms, possibly including cerebral vasoconstriction and suppression of trigeminovascular activation.

stem: New headache in a 65-year-old with scalp tenderness and jaw claudication. Immediate concern?

choices.feedback: Correct! GCA: age >50, new headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, visual symptoms (amaurosis fugax, vision loss). Elevated ESR (often >50) and CRP. START HIGH-DOSE STEROIDS IMMEDIATELY — do NOT wait for biopsy (prevents irreversible blindness from ophthalmic artery vasculitis). Temporal artery biopsy confirms diagnosis.
New headache at 65 with these features is not typical migraine.
Tension headache doesn't cause jaw claudication or scalp tenderness in this pattern.
MOH presents differently — progressive daily headache from analgesic overuse.

explanation: A new headache in a 65-year-old with scalp tenderness and jaw claudication is temporal arteritis (giant cell arteritis) until proven otherwise. This is a medical emergency — untreated GCA can cause permanent bilateral blindness from anterior ischemic optic neuropathy. Immediate high-dose corticosteroids should be started before awaiting temporal artery biopsy results. ESR and CRP should be obtained. GCA affects patients >50 almost exclusively.

stem: Triptans are CONTRAINDICATED in:

choices.text: Coronary artery disease, uncontrolled HTN, hemiplegic migraine
choices.feedback: Correct! Triptans are 5-HT1B/1D agonists that cause vasoconstriction. Contraindicated in: CAD, uncontrolled HTN, prior stroke/TIA, hemiplegic or basilar migraine, peripheral vascular disease, concurrent ergotamines or MAOIs. Risk: coronary vasospasm, MI, stroke.
Photophobia is a migraine symptom — triptans are the treatment for migraine.
Triptans are category C — can be used with caution. They're not absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy.
Some triptans are approved for adolescents. Age alone is not a contraindication.

explanation: Triptans are contraindicated in CAD, uncontrolled hypertension, and hemiplegic migraine due to their vasoconstrictive mechanism (5-HT1B/1D agonism). In hemiplegic migraine, vasoconstriction risks cerebral ischemia during the aura phase. Alternative treatments for these patients include gepants (CGRP receptor antagonists) and lasmiditan (5-HT1F agonist without vasoconstrictive activity).

stem: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri) classic patient?

choices.text: Child with fever and neck stiffness
choices.feedback: This suggests meningitis, not IIH.

choices.text: Obese young woman with headache, papilledema, and elevated LP opening pressure
choices.feedback: Correct! Classic: young obese woman (often on tetracyclines, vitamin A, or OCP) with headache, papilledema, visual changes (enlarged blind spot), pulsatile tinnitus. LP: opening pressure >25 cmH2O with normal CSF composition. MRI: normal (or empty sella, flattened posterior sclera). Treatment: weight loss, acetazolamide, serial LP, optic nerve sheath fenestration if vision threatened.
This pattern suggests normal pressure hydrocephalus or dementia.
IIH predominantly affects young obese women.

explanation: IIH (pseudotumor cerebri) classically affects obese young women with headache, papilledema, and elevated LP opening pressure (>25 cmH2O) with normal CSF and neuroimaging. Treatment includes weight loss, acetazolamide, and surgical options for refractory cases. Vision monitoring is essential as untreated IIH can cause permanent visual field loss.

stem: First-line treatment for trigeminal neuralgia?

choices.feedback: Amitriptyline is used for tension headache and migraine prophylaxis.

Correct! Carbamazepine (sodium channel blocker) is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia. Voltage-dependent sodium channel stabilization reduces neuronal hyperexcitability. Oxcarbazepine is an alternative with fewer side effects. For refractory: microvascular decompression (surgical — addresses vascular compression of CN V). MRI with CISS/FIESTA sequence to evaluate for compression.
Triptans treat migraine and cluster headache, not TN.
Verapamil is used for cluster headache prophylaxis.

explanation: Carbamazepine is the first-line treatment for trigeminal neuralgia (TN), with ~70% initial response rate. TN presents with sudden, severe, electric shock-like facial pain in the V2/V3 distribution triggered by touch, chewing, or wind. Oxcarbazepine is an alternative with fewer drug interactions. If medications fail, options include microvascular decompression (for vascular compression of CN V), gamma knife radiosurgery, or percutaneous ablative procedures.

stem: SNOOP red flags in headache — the 'O' for Onset refers to:

choices.text: Sudden onset thunderclap headache (SAH until proven otherwise)
choices.feedback: Correct! Thunderclap headache = maximal intensity within seconds. SAH must be excluded: CT head (95-98% sensitive within 6h) → LP if CT negative (xanthochromia). Other causes of thunderclap: cerebral venous thrombosis, pituitary apoplexy, cervical artery dissection, RCVS. Never dismiss a thunderclap headache as benign.
Exertional headache can be concerning but 'O' specifically refers to sudden/thunderclap onset.
While concerning, the SNOOP 'O' refers to sudden onset.
New headache after age 50 (the second 'O' in SNOOP) is the age concern.

explanation: In the SNOOP mnemonic for headache red flags, O for Onset refers to sudden-onset thunderclap headache — reaching maximum intensity within seconds to minutes. This must be treated as subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) until proven otherwise, requiring emergent non-contrast CT head (sensitivity ~98% within 6 hours) followed by lumbar puncture if CT is negative (looking for xanthochromia). SNOOP: Systemic symptoms/Secondary risk factors, Neurologic signs, Onset sudden, Older age (>50 new onset), Prior headache pattern change.

stem: Most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults?

choices.feedback: Correct! S. pneumoniae is the #1 cause of bacterial meningitis in adults overall. Highest mortality of all bacterial causes (~20-30%). N. meningitidis is #1 in adolescents and associated with petechial rash + waterhouse-friderichsen (adrenal hemorrhage). S. pneumoniae also #1 in elderly.
N. meningitidis is #1 in adolescents/young adults but not overall adults.
Listeria is important in neonates, elderly, immunocompromised, and pregnant but not #1 overall.
Dramatically reduced since Hib vaccination.

explanation: Streptococcus pneumoniae is the most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults (>18 years), accounting for approximately 50% of cases. Neisseria meningitidis is second most common in young adults and adolescents. In neonates, Group B Streptococcus, E. coli, and Listeria monocytogenes predominate. In adults >50 years and immunocompromised patients, Listeria must be covered (hence the addition of ampicillin to empiric regimens). The classic triad of bacterial meningitis (headache, fever, neck stiffness) is present in only about 44% of patients, but almost all have at least 2 of 4 features (headache, fever, stiff neck, altered mental status).

stem: When should you get CT BEFORE LP?

choices.feedback: Temperature doesn't determine need for pre-LP imaging.

choices.text: Only if the patient is over 65
choices.feedback: Age alone doesn't mandate CT before LP.

choices.text: Immunocompromised, focal deficits, papilledema, altered consciousness, new seizure, CNS disease history
choices.feedback: Correct! CT before LP to rule out mass effect/risk of herniation. In these high-risk scenarios, draw blood cultures → give empiric antibiotics + dexamethasone FIRST → CT → LP when safe. NEVER delay antibiotics to get imaging or LP.

choices.text: Always — CT is required before every LP
choices.feedback: LP can be done without CT in most cases of suspected meningitis.

explanation: CT before LP is indicated when there is risk of cerebral herniation from lumbar puncture. Indications include: immunocompromised state, history of CNS disease (mass, stroke, focal infection), new-onset seizure, papilledema, focal neurological deficits, and severely altered consciousness (GCS <10). Importantly, CT should NOT delay antibiotic administration — if CT is needed, draw blood cultures and start empiric antibiotics immediately BEFORE imaging, then perform LP after CT clears the risk of herniation. In the absence of these risk factors, LP can be performed without preceding CT.

In the absence of these risk factors, LP can be performed without preceding CT.

stem: CSF glucose is LOW in which type of meningitis?

choices.text: Neither — glucose is always normal in meningitis
choices.feedback: Glucose is characteristically low in bacterial, TB, and fungal meningitis.

choices.text: Viral meningitis has the lowest glucose
choices.feedback: Viral meningitis has normal glucose. Bacterial has low glucose.
Viral meningitis has NORMAL CSF glucose.

Correct! Bacterial meningitis: low glucose (<40 mg/dL, CSF/serum ratio <0.4) because bacteria consume glucose + impaired glucose transport across inflamed meninges. Also low in TB and fungal meningitis. Viral meningitis has normal glucose. Low glucose + high PMN WBC + high protein = bacterial.

explanation: CSF glucose is characteristically LOW in bacterial meningitis (typically <40 mg/dL or CSF-to-serum glucose ratio <0.4). Bacteria and neutrophils consume glucose in the CSF. The full CSF profile in bacterial meningitis includes: elevated opening pressure, high WBC count (>1000, neutrophil-predominant), elevated protein (>250 mg/dL), and low glucose. Viral meningitis shows lymphocytic pleocytosis with normal glucose and mildly elevated protein. Tuberculous and fungal meningitis also have low glucose but with lymphocytic predominance and very high protein. Partially treated bacterial meningitis can mimic viral profiles.

stem: HSV encephalitis has predilection for which brain region?

choices.feedback: Occipital involvement suggests PRES or PCA stroke, not HSV.
Cerebellar involvement suggests different infectious or autoimmune etiologies.

Correct! HSV-1 enters CNS via olfactory or trigeminal nerve → preferentially affects temporal and frontal lobes (limbic system). Presents: fever + altered mental status + behavioral changes + seizures + aphasia. MRI: T2 hyperintensity in medial temporal lobes (often asymmetric). CSF PCR for HSV is diagnostic gold standard.
Basal ganglia involvement is more typical of hypertensive hemorrhage or Wilson disease.

explanation: Herpes simplex encephalitis (HSE) has a strong predilection for the temporal lobes (especially medial temporal and orbitofrontal cortex). This tropism is thought to relate to the virus traveling along the olfactory or trigeminal nerve pathways to these regions. MRI shows T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in the temporal lobes, often asymmetric and sometimes with hemorrhagic changes. HSE presents with fever, altered mental status, seizures, and behavioral changes (reflecting temporal lobe involvement). HSV-1 is the most common cause of sporadic fatal encephalitis. Diagnosis is confirmed by CSF HSV PCR. Treatment with IV acyclovir must begin empirically whenever HSE is suspected — delay increases mortality.

stem: Empiric treatment for suspected bacterial meningitis in a 65-year-old?

choices.feedback: This covers pneumococcus but misses Listeria, which is important in patients >50.
Insufficient coverage for the range of organisms in elderly meningitis.

Correct! Age >50, immunocompromised, alcoholic, or pregnant → add ampicillin to cover Listeria monocytogenes. Listeria is intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins. Vancomycin covers resistant pneumococcus. Also give dexamethasone BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose.
This covers anaerobes and gram-negatives but misses the key meningitis organisms.

explanation: Empiric treatment for suspected bacterial meningitis in adults ≥50 years requires ceftriaxone (covers S. pneumoniae and N. meningitidis) plus vancomycin (covers penicillin-resistant pneumococci) plus ampicillin (covers Listeria monocytogenes). Listeria coverage with ampicillin is essential in patients >50, immunocompromised patients, pregnant women, and neonates because Listeria is intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins. For patients 18-49 without immunocompromise, the standard empiric regimen is ceftriaxone plus vancomycin (no ampicillin). Dexamethasone should be given before or with the first antibiotic dose.

stem: Dexamethasone in bacterial meningitis should be given:

choices.feedback: Dexamethasone is specifically for bacterial meningitis, not viral.
Waiting defeats the purpose — dexamethasone must be given early.

choices.text: BEFORE or WITH the first antibiotic dose
choices.feedback: Correct! Dexamethasone4 days. Must be given before or simultaneously with first antibiotic — antibiotics cause bacterial lysis → massive inflammatory response → cerebral edema. Dexamethasone blunts this. Primary benefit: reduces hearing loss in pneumococcal meningitis. No benefit if given after antibiotics started.

choices.text: Only if CSF glucose is normal
choices.feedback: Dexamethasone is given empirically, not based on CSF glucose.

explanation: Dexamethasone in bacterial meningitis must be given BEFORE or WITH the first dose of antibiotics — not after. The mechanism is suppression of the inflammatory cascade triggered by bacterial lysis (which releases cell wall components that drive inflammation). If given after antibiotics have already lysed bacteria and initiated the inflammatory response, dexamethasone provides no benefit. Evidence is strongest for S. pneumoniae meningitis, where dexamethasone reduces mortality and hearing loss. The typical regimen is dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours for 4 days. If the organism turns out not to be S. pneumoniae, some guidelines suggest discontinuing dexamethasone.

stem: Close contacts of N. meningitidis meningitis should receive:

choices.feedback: IV antibiotics are for treatment, not prophylaxis of contacts.

Correct! Chemoprophylaxis for close contacts (household, kissing contacts, exposure to secretions): rifampin2 days, OR ciprofloxacin1 dose, OR ceftriaxone1 dose (preferred in pregnancy). Give within 24h of diagnosis. Also vaccinate if serogroup covered by available vaccine.
Close contacts have significantly elevated risk and need prophylaxis.
Steroids are not used for contact prophylaxis.

explanation: Close contacts of a patient with Neisseria meningitidis meningitis should receive chemoprophylaxis to prevent secondary cases. Options include rifampin (600 mg PO q12h for 2 days), ciprofloxacin (single 500 mg PO dose — often preferred for convenience), or ceftriaxone (single 250 mg IM dose — preferred in pregnancy). Prophylaxis should be given to household contacts, childcare/nursery contacts, anyone with direct exposure to respiratory secretions (kissing, mouth-to-mouth resuscitation), and healthcare workers who performed intubation or suctioning without a mask. Casual contacts (same workplace, school classroom) generally do NOT require prophylaxis.

stem: A patient with fever, altered mental status, and seizures has MRI showing T2 hyperintensity in bilateral temporal lobes. First action?

choices.feedback: NEVER wait for PCR — HSV encephalitis mortality is ~70% untreated. Start acyclovir immediately.
Bacterial meningitis antibiotics don't cover HSV. The temporal lobe MRI is classic for HSV encephalitis.

Correct! HSV encephalitis is the most likely diagnosis (fever + AMS + seizures + temporal lobe MRI findings). Start IV acyclovir14-21 days IMMEDIATELY. Early treatment reduces mortality from ~70% to <20%. Send CSF for HSV PCR but do NOT delay treatment.

choices.text: Observe and repeat MRI in 48 hours
choices.feedback: Observation allows disease progression and increases mortality.

explanation: Fever, altered mental status, seizures, and MRI showing temporal lobe involvement is the classic presentation of HSV encephalitis. IV acyclovir must be started empirically and immediately — this is a time-critical diagnosis where delay in treatment dramatically increases mortality (from ~70% untreated to ~20% with treatment, though many survivors have significant neurological sequelae). LP with CSF HSV PCR confirms the diagnosis, but treatment should never be delayed pending results. CSF in HSE typically shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, normal glucose, and sometimes RBCs (from hemorrhagic necrosis of temporal lobes).

stem: Most common initial presentation of MS?

choices.feedback: Transverse myelitis can occur but is not the most common initial presentation.
Seizures are uncommon in MS (cortical gray matter is relatively spared).
Intention tremor can occur in MS (cerebellar) but is not the most common initial symptom.

Correct! Optic neuritis is the most common initial MS presentation — unilateral vision loss, pain with eye movement, afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil). ~50% of optic neuritis patients develop MS within 15 years. MRI brain at presentation determines risk.

explanation: Correct: Optic neuritis (unilateral vision loss with pain on eye movement).

stem: Bilateral INO in a young adult is highly suggestive of:

choices.feedback: Tumors can cause INO but bilateral INO in a young adult strongly suggests MS.
MG causes fatigable ptosis and diplopia but not INO specifically.
GBS is a peripheral nerve disease; INO is a CNS brainstem finding.

Correct! Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) from MLF lesion: impaired adduction ipsilateral + abducting nystagmus contralateral. Bilateral INO in a young adult = MS until proven otherwise. In elderly: most commonly caused by brainstem stroke.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Multiple sclerosis) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: MS symptoms worsening with heat exposure is called:

choices.feedback: Lhermitte = electric shock sensation with neck flexion (posterior column).

Correct! Uhthoff phenomenon: heat slows conduction velocity in demyelinated nerves → transient worsening of existing MS symptoms. Classic: vision worsens after hot bath, exercise, or fever. Pseudoexacerbation (not a true relapse). Symptoms resolve with cooling.
Romberg tests proprioception (dorsal column) — positive when balance worsens with eyes closed.
Babinski (upgoing plantar response) indicates UMN lesion — can be present in MS but is not specific to heat.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Uhthoff phenomenon) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: CSF finding most specific for MS?

choices.feedback: Xanthochromia indicates prior subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Low CSF glucose = bacterial meningitis or TB meningitis, not MS.

choices.text: Oligoclonal bands (IgG) present in CSF but not serum
choices.feedback: Correct! Oligoclonal bands (OCBs) present in CSF but absent in serum indicate intrathecal IgG synthesis — present in >95% of MS patients. Not 100% specific (also seen in neurosyphilis, Lyme, SSPE), but in the right clinical context, strongly supports MS diagnosis. Elevated IgG index is also supportive.
That's albuminocytologic dissociation — classic for GBS.

explanation: Bacterial meningitis presents with the classic triad of fever, nuchal rigidity, and altered mental status (though all three are present in <50% of cases). CSF findings: elevated WBC with neutrophilic predominance, elevated protein, and low glucose (<40 mg/dL or CSF:serum ratio <0.4). Empiric treatment: ceftriaxone + vancomycin (± ampicillin for age >50 or immunocompromised to cover Listeria). Dexamethasone given before or with the first antibiotic dose reduces mortality in pneumococcal meningitis. Do not delay antibiotics for imaging if clinical suspicion is high.

stem: Acute MS relapse treatment?

choices.text: IV methylprednisolone pulse therapy for 3-5 days
choices.feedback: Correct! High-dose IV steroids shorten relapse duration and speed recovery. Does NOT change long-term disease progression or prevent future relapses. For steroid-refractory relapses: plasmapheresis (PLEX). Long-term disease modification requires DMTs, not steroids.
Natalizumab is a DMT for relapse prevention, not acute relapse treatment.
IFN-β prevents future relapses (DMT) but does not treat acute attacks.
Low-dose oral steroids are less effective than IV pulse therapy for MS relapses.

explanation: Multiple sclerosis diagnosis requires dissemination in space (≥2 CNS regions affected) and time (≥2 episodes separated in time). McDonald criteria (2017 revision) allow MRI to substitute for clinical episodes. CSF oligoclonal bands support the diagnosis. Acute relapses: high-dose IV methylprednisolone. Disease-modifying therapy: interferons, glatiramer acetate, natalizumab, ocrelizumab, fingolimod. MRI shows periventricular white matter lesions (Dawson's fingers). Optic neuritis is a common presenting symptom.

stem: Which DMT carries risk of PML from JC virus reactivation?

choices.feedback: IFN-β does not carry significant PML risk.
DMF has rare PML reports but it's primarily associated with natalizumab.
Glatiramer has a very favorable safety profile with no PML risk.

Correct! Natalizumab (α4-integrin antibody) prevents lymphocyte CNS entry — highly effective but carries PML risk (JC virus reactivation). Risk factors: JCV antibody positive, treatment >2 years, prior immunosuppression. Screen JCV antibody before starting. Consider switching after 2 years if JCV+.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Natalizumab) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: Ocrelizumab is significant because it is:

choices.feedback: Ocrelizumab is effective in both sexes.
No DMT cures MS. They reduce relapse frequency and slow disability progression.
First oral MS drug was fingolimod. Ocrelizumab is IV infusion.

choices.text: The first DMT approved for primary progressive MS (PPMS)
choices.feedback: Correct! Ocrelizumab (anti-CD20, B-cell depleting) is the FIRST and currently only DMT with proven benefit in PPMS (ORATORIO trial). Also highly effective in RRMS. PPMS has historically been treatment-resistant, making this a landmark approval.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (The first DMT approved for primary progressive MS (PPMS)) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: MS lesion on MRI that enhances with gadolinium indicates:

choices.feedback: Normal white matter does not enhance with gadolinium.
Chronic plaques show T2 hyperintensity but do NOT enhance with gadolinium.

Correct! Gadolinium enhancement = breakdown of blood-brain barrier = active inflammation. Enhancing lesions represent new or actively inflamed plaques (within ~6 weeks). Non-enhancing T2 lesions = older/chronic plaques. Simultaneous enhancing + non-enhancing lesions on single MRI can satisfy dissemination in time criterion.
While tumors enhance, ring enhancement and clinical context distinguish them from MS plaques.

explanation: Correct: Active inflammation (recent or ongoing demyelination).

stem: Hallmark feature that distinguishes MG from other causes of weakness?

choices.text: Weakness is constant throughout the day
choices.feedback: Constant weakness suggests other myopathies. MG fluctuates.

choices.text: Fatigable weakness — worsens with use, improves with rest
choices.feedback: Correct! Fatigability is the hallmark of MG. Antibodies block AChR → fewer functional receptors → repeated stimulation depletes available ACh → progressive weakness. Rest allows ACh to rebuild. Classic: ptosis worsens with sustained upgaze, voice fades during conversation, difficulty chewing at end of meal.
Improvement with use = Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (pre-synaptic, facilitating).
Elevated CK with proximal weakness = inflammatory myopathy (polymyositis/dermatomyositis).

explanation: Myasthenia gravis is caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors (AChR-Ab in 85%) or muscle-specific kinase (MuSK in 5-8%). Fatigable weakness worsening with repetitive use is the hallmark. Diagnosis: AChR antibodies, decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation, edrophonium (Tensilon) test. Treatment: pyridostigmine (symptomatic), immunosuppression (corticosteroids, azathioprine), thymectomy for thymoma or generalized MG. Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure) requires ICU, IVIG or plasmapheresis, and avoiding aminoglycosides/beta-blockers.

stem: Most SENSITIVE diagnostic test for MG?

choices.feedback: Most specific but only ~85% sensitive for generalized MG and 50% for ocular MG.
Historically used but less sensitive and rarely performed now due to cardiac risks.
CT evaluates for thymoma but doesn't diagnose MG.

Correct! Single-fiber EMG detects increased 'jitter' (variability in neuromuscular transmission time) — most sensitive test (>95%). Used when clinical suspicion is high but antibodies and RNS are negative. Repetitive nerve stimulation (decremental response) is more commonly used but less sensitive.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Single-fiber EMG) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: ALL MG patients should get which imaging study?

choices.feedback: MG is not associated with abdominal pathology.

Correct! 10% of MG patients have thymoma, 65% have thymic hyperplasia. ALL patients need CT chest at diagnosis. Thymoma requires surgical resection regardless. Even without thymoma, thymectomy is beneficial in generalized AChR+ MG (MGTX trial — improved outcomes over 3 years).
MRI spine evaluates for spinal cord pathology, not NMJ disease.
MG is a peripheral NMJ disorder, not a CNS disease.

explanation: Correct: CT chest (to evaluate for thymoma).

stem: Lambert-Eaton vs MG — key distinction?

choices.feedback: Correct! LEMS = pre-synaptic (VGCC antibodies block calcium-mediated ACh release). Repeated stimulation → calcium accumulates → MORE ACh released → strength improves (facilitating response). Associated with small cell lung cancer (paraneoplastic). RNS shows INCREMENTAL response. MG = post-synaptic, decremental response, associated with thymoma.
LEMS treatment includes 3,4-DAP (amifampridine) to increase ACh release, plus treating underlying cancer.
LEMS typically causes proximal limb weakness, autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth), and hyporeflexia.

choices.text: LEMS has autoantibodies against AChR receptors
choices.feedback: LEMS has anti-VGCC antibodies (pre-synaptic), not anti-AChR.

explanation: Correct: LEMS: weakness IMPROVES with use (pre-synaptic VGCC antibodies, associated with SCLC).

stem: Drugs that can worsen MG include all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Aminoglycosides worsen MG — they impair neuromuscular transmission.
Magnesium impairs neuromuscular transmission and can precipitate myasthenic crisis.

Correct! Pyridostigmine is the primary TREATMENT for MG (AChE inhibitor → increases ACh at NMJ). Drugs that worsen MG: aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, magnesium, D-penicillamine, botulinum toxin. Always check the drug list before prescribing to MG patients.
Fluoroquinolones can worsen MG and carry a boxed warning.

explanation: Myasthenia gravis is caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors (AChR-Ab in 85%) or muscle-specific kinase (MuSK in 5-8%). Fatigable weakness worsening with repetitive use is the hallmark. Diagnosis: AChR antibodies, decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation, edrophonium (Tensilon) test. Treatment: pyridostigmine (symptomatic), immunosuppression (corticosteroids, azathioprine), thymectomy for thymoma or generalized MG. Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure) requires ICU, IVIG or plasmapheresis, and avoiding aminoglycosides/beta-blockers.

stem: #1 trigger for myasthenic crisis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Infection (especially respiratory) is the most common precipitant of myasthenic crisis. The inflammatory response and physiologic stress exacerbate NMJ dysfunction. Always workup and treat infection in crisis. Other triggers: surgery, medication changes, certain drugs (aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones).
Exercise exacerbates fatigable weakness but rarely triggers full crisis without another factor.
Temperature can affect NMJ function but is not the primary trigger.
Stress can worsen MG but is not the #1 trigger.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Infection) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: Cholinergic crisis from excess pyridostigmine presents with:

choices.feedback: Cholinergic excess causes bradycardia and hypotension, not the reverse.
Fever + rigidity suggests NMS or serotonin syndrome, not cholinergic crisis.

choices.text: DUMBBELSS (diarrhea, urination, miosis, bronchospasm, bradycardia, emesis, lacrimation, salivation, sweating) + weakness
choices.feedback: Correct! Excess AChE inhibitor → too much ACh at muscarinic AND nicotinic receptors. Muscarinic: DUMBBELSS. Nicotinic: fasciculations + weakness (depolarization block). Distinguishing from myasthenic crisis is critical — both cause weakness, but cholinergic crisis has cholinergic signs. Treatment: stop pyridostigmine, atropine for muscarinic effects.
Those are anticholinergic effects — the OPPOSITE of cholinergic excess.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (DUMBBELSS (diarrhea, urination, miosis, bronchospasm, bradycardia, emesis, lacrimation, salivation, sweating) + weakness) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: Hallmark clinical feature of myasthenia gravis?

choices.feedback: That's GBS. MG has a different pattern.

choices.text: Fixed weakness worse in the morning
choices.feedback: MG is typically worse later in the day with sustained activity.
Resting tremor is characteristic of Parkinson disease.

choices.text: Fatigable weakness that worsens with use
choices.feedback: Correct! Fatigable weakness is pathognomonic for MG. Ptosis worsens through the day or with sustained upward gaze. Improvement after rest distinguishes MG from other neuromuscular conditions. Ice pack test: ptosis improves with cold application (reduces AChE activity at NMJ).

explanation: Best answer: Fatigable weakness that worsens with use and improves with rest.

stem: Most common antibody in myasthenia gravis?

choices.feedback: Anti-Jo1 is associated with antisynthetase syndrome/inflammatory myopathy.

Correct! Anti-AChR antibodies in 85% of generalized MG. They block, cross-link, and destroy postsynaptic receptors via complement activation. Most specific diagnostic test. If negative, check anti-MuSK. All MG patients need CT chest to screen for thymoma.
Anti-dsDNA is specific for SLE.
Anti-MuSK is found in 5-8%, typically with more severe bulbar symptoms.

explanation: Correct: Anti-AChR (acetylcholine receptor).

stem: #1 trigger for myasthenic crisis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Infection (especially respiratory) is the most common precipitant of myasthenic crisis. The inflammatory response and physiologic stress exacerbate NMJ dysfunction. Always workup and treat infection in crisis. Other triggers: surgery, medication changes, certain drugs (aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones).
Exercise exacerbates fatigable weakness but rarely triggers full crisis without another factor.
Temperature can affect NMJ function but is not the primary trigger.
Stress can worsen MG but is not the #1 trigger.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Infection) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: Medications that can WORSEN myasthenia gravis include:

choices.feedback: Standard analgesics do not affect NMJ transmission.
These are treatments for MG, not exacerbating medications.

Correct! These drugs worsen NMJ transmission. Aminoglycosides/fluoroquinolones: impair presynaptic ACh release. Beta-blockers: worsen fatigue. Magnesium: blocks NMJ calcium channels. Also: succinylcholine (prolonged paralysis), D-penicillamine, phenytoin, telithromycin.
These medications are safe in MG patients.

explanation: Myasthenia gravis is caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors (AChR-Ab in 85%) or muscle-specific kinase (MuSK in 5-8%). Fatigable weakness worsening with repetitive use is the hallmark. Diagnosis: AChR antibodies, decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation, edrophonium (Tensilon) test. Treatment: pyridostigmine (symptomatic), immunosuppression (corticosteroids, azathioprine), thymectomy for thymoma or generalized MG. Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure) requires ICU, IVIG or plasmapheresis, and avoiding aminoglycosides/beta-blockers.

stem: DUMBBELSS symptoms indicate:

choices.feedback: Myasthenic crisis = weakness from insufficient treatment. DUMBBELSS = excessive cholinergic stimulation.
Serotonin syndrome: hyperthermia, rigidity, clonus, agitation.

Correct! Cholinergic crisis from excessive pyridostigmine: Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bradycardia, Bronchospasm/Bronchorrhea, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation, Sweating. Treatment: hold AChE inhibitors, atropine for muscarinic symptoms, supportive care.
Organophosphates cause similar cholinergic symptoms but from external toxin, not overtreatment.

explanation: Correct: Cholinergic crisis (overtreatment with AChE inhibitors).

stem: Repetitive nerve stimulation in MG shows:

choices.feedback: Conduction block is seen in GBS/CIDP demyelinating conditions.
Incrementing response is seen in Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome.
Fibrillation potentials suggest denervation (ALS, radiculopathy), not MG.

Correct! Low-frequency (2-3 Hz) repetitive nerve stimulation: progressive decrease in compound muscle action potential amplitude >10% = decremental response. This reflects progressive depletion of available AChR at the NMJ. Lambert-Eaton shows INCREMENTAL response at HIGH frequency (post-tetanic facilitation).

explanation: Myasthenia gravis is caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors (AChR-Ab in 85%) or muscle-specific kinase (MuSK in 5-8%). Fatigable weakness worsening with repetitive use is the hallmark. Diagnosis: AChR antibodies, decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation, edrophonium (Tensilon) test. Treatment: pyridostigmine (symptomatic), immunosuppression (corticosteroids, azathioprine), thymectomy for thymoma or generalized MG. Myasthenic crisis (respiratory failure) requires ICU, IVIG or plasmapheresis, and avoiding aminoglycosides/beta-blockers.

stem: Thymectomy in MG is recommended for:

choices.feedback: Thymectomy benefits even non-thymoma MG patients.

choices.text: All thymoma patients AND non-thymoma generalized MG
choices.feedback: Correct! MGTX trial: thymectomy + prednisone was superior to prednisone alone in non-thymoma generalized AChR+ MG (ages 18-65). All thymoma patients should have thymectomy regardless. CT chest screening is mandatory for all MG patients.

choices.text: Only patients who fail all other treatments
choices.feedback: Thymectomy is recommended early, not as a last resort.
Seronegative patients may benefit but the strongest evidence is for AChR+ MG.

explanation: Correct: All thymoma patients AND non-thymoma generalized MG (ages 18-65).

stem: In myasthenic crisis, which treatments provide rapid improvement?

choices.feedback: Azathioprine takes 3-6 months for full effect — too slow for crisis.
Steroids take weeks to work and can initially worsen weakness.
Excessive pyridostigmine can cause cholinergic crisis. Typically held or reduced during crisis.

Correct! Both provide rapid improvement (days). IVIG: weight-based dosing × 5 days. Plasmapheresis: removes circulating antibodies. Choose one (don't combine — PLEX removes IVIG). Also: intubate if FVC <target rate, treat trigger (infection), hold/reduce pyridostigmine (may worsen secretions during crisis).

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (IVIG or plasmapheresis) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: The tetrad of NMS (FARM) includes:

choices.feedback: These are nonspecific systemic symptoms, not NMS.
Ataxia, rash, and myoclonus are not core features of NMS.

choices.text: Fever, Anemia, Renal failure, Mucosal bleeding
choices.feedback: This describes TTP/HUS-like features, not NMS.

Correct! FARM: Fever (often >40°C), Autonomic instability (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis), Rigidity (generalized lead-pipe — distinguishes from serotonin syndrome), Mental status changes (confusion → obtundation → coma). CK is markedly elevated.

explanation: Key detail: pipe), Mental status changes.

stem: What MOST reliably distinguishes NMS from serotonin syndrome?

choices.feedback: Both NMS and serotonin syndrome cause hyperthermia.
Both cause altered mental status.

Correct! NMS: generalized lead-pipe rigidity with NORMAL or decreased reflexes. Serotonin syndrome: clonus (especially lower extremity), hyperreflexia, and tremor. Clonus is the single most distinguishing feature of serotonin syndrome. Also: NMS develops over days-weeks; serotonin syndrome within hours.
Both cause diaphoresis as part of autonomic dysfunction.

explanation: Correct: Lead-pipe rigidity (NMS) vs clonus/hyperreflexia (serotonin syndrome).

stem: Treatment of NMS includes:

choices.feedback: The antipsychotic is the CAUSE — it must be stopped immediately.
Cyproheptadine is the treatment for serotonin syndrome, not NMS.

Correct! Dantrolene (muscle relaxant — reduces rigidity/heat) + bromocriptine (dopamine agonist — reverses dopamine blockade). Also: stop offending agent, aggressive cooling, IV fluids, ICU monitoring. Amantadine can substitute for bromocriptine. Benzodiazepines for agitation.
Naloxone reverses opioids, not NMS.

explanation: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) is a life-threatening reaction to dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics). Tetrad: hyperthermia, lead-pipe rigidity, altered mental status, and autonomic instability. Distinguishing from serotonin syndrome: NMS has lead-pipe rigidity and bradyreflexia vs serotonin syndrome with clonus, hyperreflexia, and myoclonus. Treatment: discontinue offending agent, dantrolene (muscle relaxant), bromocriptine (dopamine agonist), aggressive cooling, supportive care. CK is markedly elevated due to rhabdomyolysis.

stem: NMS can be triggered by withdrawal of which medication?

choices.feedback: Opioid withdrawal causes GI symptoms and autonomic changes but not NMS.

Correct! Abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic agents (levodopa, dopamine agonists) in Parkinson patients can trigger NMS-like syndrome. Same pathophysiology: sudden central dopamine depletion. Never abruptly stop Parkinson medications. Treat the same as NMS + resume dopaminergic therapy.
SSRI withdrawal causes discontinuation syndrome, not NMS.
Benzodiazepine withdrawal causes seizures and autonomic instability but not NMS.

explanation: Correct: Levodopa (in Parkinson disease).

stem: Masseter spasm during induction of anesthesia suggests:

choices.feedback: Cholinergic crisis has DUMBBELSS symptoms, not masseter spasm during anesthesia.
Serotonin syndrome is caused by serotonergic drugs, not anesthetics.
NMS develops over days, not acutely during anesthesia.

Correct! Masseter spasm (trismus) during anesthesia, especially with succinylcholine, is an early warning of malignant hyperthermia. MH: genetic (RYR1 mutation, autosomal dominant), triggered by volatile anesthetics + succinylcholine. Treatment: stop triggers, dantrolene IV, active cooling.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Malignant hyperthermia) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: CK elevation in NMS is typically:

choices.text: Elevated only if patient has seizures
choices.feedback: CK is elevated from sustained muscle rigidity, not seizures (NMS rarely causes seizures).
Normal CK essentially rules out NMS.

Correct! Massive CK elevation from generalized muscle rigidity → rhabdomyolysis. CK >1000 is common; levels >100,000 occur in severe cases. Rhabdomyolysis → myoglobinuria → AKI (leading cause of death in NMS). Aggressive IV hydration is essential for renal protection.
Mild elevation is more typical of statin myopathy. NMS causes severe elevation.

explanation: Correct: Markedly elevated (often >1000, can reach >100,000).

stem: After NMS resolves, can the patient ever take antipsychotics again?

choices.feedback: Re-challenge is possible with careful precautions.

choices.text: Yes — after 2+ weeks, using a low
choices.feedback: Correct! Re-challenge is reasonable after full NMS resolution (minimum 2 weeks, ideally longer). Use a different antipsychotic (low-potency typical or atypical, different class from trigger), start at low dose with very slow titration, close monitoring. Recurrence risk: ~10-30%. Shared decision-making with patient.
Route of administration doesn't change NMS risk — it's the dopamine blockade mechanism.

choices.text: Only if CK returns to normal within 24 hours
choices.feedback: CK normalization takes days-weeks. Re-challenge timing is based on clinical resolution, not just CK.

explanation: Key detail: potency or atypical agent with slow titration.

stem: Dantrolene works by:

choices.feedback: Bromocriptine is the dopamine agonist. Dantrolene works peripherally on muscle.
AChE inhibition is the mechanism of pyridostigmine (treats MG) and nerve agents.

choices.text: Blocking calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum in skeletal muscle
choices.feedback: Correct! Dantrolene inhibits ryanodine receptor (RYR1) → reduces intracellular calcium release → decreases muscle contraction → reduces rigidity and heat generation. Used in both NMS and malignant hyperthermia. Side effects: hepatotoxicity, muscle weakness. Only acts on skeletal muscle — does not affect cardiac or smooth muscle.
That's the mechanism of benzodiazepines and barbiturates.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Blocking calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum in skeletal muscle) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: Status epilepticus is defined as continuous seizure activity lasting:

choices.feedback: This was the old definition. Current operational definition is 5 minutes to encourage earlier intervention.
Most seizures self-terminate by 1-2 minutes. SE threshold is longer.
Most normal seizures last 30-120 seconds.

Correct! ≥5 minutes of continuous seizure activity OR ≥2 seizures without returning to baseline between them. The old definition was 30 minutes, but the 5-minute operational definition allows earlier treatment since seizures rarely self-terminate after 5 minutes and prolonged activity causes neuronal damage.

explanation: Status epilepticus is a neurologic emergency defined as continuous seizure activity >5 minutes or recurrent seizures without return to baseline. First-line: IV benzodiazepines (lorazepamor midazolam). Second-line: IV fosphenytoin, valproate, or levetiracetam. Third-line (refractory SE): IV anesthetics (propofol, midazolam infusion, or pentobarbital) with continuous EEG monitoring. Seizure classification (ILAE 2017): focal (aware or impaired awareness), generalized (tonic-clonic, absence, myoclonic), or unknown onset.

stem: First-line treatment for status epilepticus?

choices.feedback: Correct! Benzodiazepines are first-line. IV lorazepam(preferred IV — longer brain duration than diazepam) or IM midazolam(if no IV access — RAMPART trial showed non-inferior outcomes and faster administration). Can repeat lorazepam × 1 if seizure continues.
Phenytoin/fosphenytoin is second-line, given after benzodiazepines fail.
Oral medications are too slow to absorb during active SE.
Valproate is a second-line option after benzodiazepines.

explanation: Status epilepticus is a neurologic emergency defined as continuous seizure activity >5 minutes or recurrent seizures without return to baseline. First-line: IV benzodiazepines (lorazepamor midazolam). Second-line: IV fosphenytoin, valproate, or levetiracetam. Third-line (refractory SE): IV anesthetics (propofol, midazolam infusion, or pentobarbital) with continuous EEG monitoring. Seizure classification (ILAE 2017): focal (aware or impaired awareness), generalized (tonic-clonic, absence, myoclonic), or unknown onset.

stem: Most common cause of SE in adults?

choices.feedback: Alcohol withdrawal (not intoxication) causes seizures. Not the most common overall.
Thyroid disorders rarely cause SE.
Tumors cause seizures but are not the #1 cause of SE.

Correct! Non-compliance with or subtherapeutic levels of anti-epileptic drugs is the most common cause of SE in known epilepsy patients. Always check AED levels. Other common causes: CNS infection, stroke, metabolic derangements (hypoglycemia, hyponatremia), alcohol withdrawal, drug toxicity.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (AED non-compliance/subtherapeutic levels) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: RAMPART trial demonstrated that IM midazolam is:

choices.feedback: RAMPART included adults. IM midazolam is effective across age groups.

choices.text: Non-inferior to IV lorazepam and faster to administer
choices.feedback: Correct! RAMPART (2012): IM midazolam was non-inferior (and actually showed a numerical advantage) compared to IV lorazepam for pre-hospital SE. Key advantage: no IV access needed → faster administration in the field. Changed practice for EMS protocols.
IM midazolam works for generalized convulsive SE, the most dangerous type.
RAMPART showed IM midazolam was at least as effective.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Non-inferior to IV lorazepam and faster to administer) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: Fosphenytoin is preferred over phenytoin because:

choices.feedback: Fosphenytoin is given IV or IM.

choices.text: It does not require cardiac monitoring
choices.feedback: Both require cardiac monitoring during infusion.

choices.text: It is more effective at stopping seizures
choices.feedback: Efficacy is equivalent. The advantage is in the safety profile.

choices.text: It causes less hypotension and avoids Purple Glove Syndrome
choices.feedback: Correct! Fosphenytoin (prodrug of phenytoin) advantages: can be infused faster, can be given IM, does not cause Purple Glove Syndrome (tissue necrosis from phenytoin extravasation — alkaline pH damages tissue). Both can cause hypotension and cardiac arrhythmias — monitor on telemetry during infusion.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (It causes less hypotension and avoids Purple Glove Syndrome) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: Non-convulsive SE should be suspected in:

choices.feedback: NCSE by definition involves altered consciousness.

choices.text: Patients with unexplained altered mental status who don't wake up post-ictally
choices.feedback: Correct! NCSE: altered consciousness WITHOUT motor manifestations. Suspect when: post-ictal patient doesn't recover, unexplained AMS in ICU, subtle signs (eye deviation, nystagmus, eyelid fluttering). Requires continuous EEG to diagnose. Commonly missed — has significant morbidity if untreated.
Syncope has rapid recovery and different EEG findings from NCSE.

choices.text: Patients with headache and normal EEG
choices.feedback: Normal EEG rules out NCSE. Headache alone is not suggestive.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Patients with unexplained altered mental status who don't wake up post-ictally) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: ESETT trial showed that for second-line SE treatment:

choices.feedback: All three work in about half of benzodiazepine-refractory cases.

choices.text: Fosphenytoin, levetiracetam, and valproate are equally effective (~47%)
choices.feedback: Correct! ESETT (2019): all three second-line agents were equally effective at ~47% seizure cessation rate. This validated levetiracetam and valproate as equivalent alternatives to the traditional fosphenytoin. Choice based on: drug interactions, hepatic/renal function, patient factors. Levetiracetam gaining favor due to fewer interactions.

choices.text: Phenytoin is clearly superior to all alternatives
choices.feedback: ESETT showed no significant difference among the three agents.
All three agents were about 47% effective — meaning >50% of patients needed third-line treatment.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Fosphenytoin, levetiracetam, and valproate are equally effective (~47%)) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: Refractory SE requires:

choices.feedback: Refractory SE will NOT self-terminate and causes progressive neuronal damage.
Surgery is for refractory epilepsy, not acute SE management.
Oral medications are insufficient. Refractory SE needs IV anesthetics.

choices.text: Continuous IV anesthetics (midazolam, propofol, or pentobarbital) with intubation + EEG monitoring
choices.feedback: Correct! Refractory SE (fails 2 appropriately dosed medications) requires: intubation for airway protection, continuous IV anesthetic infusion, continuous EEG monitoring targeting burst suppression. Options: midazolam (fewest hemodynamic effects), propofol (fast onset — watch for propofol infusion syndrome with prolonged use), pentobarbital (most potent but most hemodynamic compromise).

explanation: Best answer: Continuous IV anesthetics (midazolam, propofol, or pentobarbital) with intubation + EEG monitoring.

stem: Most common type of stroke?

choices.feedback: SAH accounts for ~5%.
ICH accounts for ~10% of strokes.
Correct! Ischemic strokes account for ~85% of all strokes. Caused by thrombotic or embolic arterial occlusion. MCA territory is most commonly affected.
Rare cause of stroke.

explanation: Ischemic stroke accounts for approximately 87% of all strokes and is caused by thrombotic or embolic occlusion of a cerebral artery. Hemorrhagic stroke (intracerebral hemorrhage and subarachnoid hemorrhage) accounts for the remaining 13%. The middle cerebral artery (MCA) is the most commonly affected vessel. Ischemic stroke subtypes are classified by the TOAST system: large-artery atherosclerosis, cardioembolism (often from atrial fibrillation), small-vessel occlusion (lacunar), stroke of other determined etiology, and stroke of undetermined etiology. Venous sinus thrombosis is a rare cause of stroke, accounting for less than 1% of cases.

stem: IV tPA window for ischemic stroke?

choices.feedback: Time window is critical — benefit decreases with delay.
tPA is not given after 4.5h. Thrombectomy may extend to 24h for select LVO.

Correct! IV alteplase within 4.5h (ECASS-3 trial extended from 3h). Door-to-needle goal ≤60 min. Must exclude hemorrhage (non-contrast CT) and check glucose first. BP must be <185/110 before tPA. Mechanical thrombectomy extends window to 24h for LVO with perfusion mismatch.
Too restrictive — benefit extends further.

explanation: IV alteplase (tPA) for acute ischemic stroke must be administered within 4.5 hours of symptom onset (or last known well time). The earlier treatment is given, the better the outcome — the benefit decreases significantly with each passing hour. For patients with large vessel occlusion (LVO), endovascular mechanical thrombectomy can be performed up to 6 hours from onset based on multiple 2015 trials, and up to 24 hours in select patients with favorable perfusion imaging (salvageable penumbra). Key tPA exclusions include active bleeding, recent surgery, platelet count below threshold, INR above threshold, and uncontrolled hypertension above the treatment threshold.

stem: What must be checked BEFORE giving tPA?

choices.feedback: LP is for SAH workup (if CT negative), not before tPA.
Troponin evaluates cardiac injury, not stroke management priority.
MRI is more sensitive but too time-consuming. Non-contrast CT is sufficient to exclude hemorrhage before tPA.

Correct! Hypoglycemia is the most important stroke mimic to exclude — it can cause focal neurologic deficits identical to stroke. Check glucose FIRST. Glucose <50 is a contraindication to tPA. Also need: non-contrast CT (exclude hemorrhage), BP (<185/110), and coagulation studies.

explanation: Blood glucose must be checked before administering tPA because hypoglycemia is a common stroke mimic that causes focal neurological deficits indistinguishable from ischemic stroke. Treating a hypoglycemic patient with tPA exposes them to bleeding risk without benefit. Blood glucose is the ONLY lab result that must be available before tPA administration — all other labs (CBC, coags, chemistry) can be drawn but should not delay treatment unless there is clinical suspicion of coagulopathy or thrombocytopenia. Non-contrast CT head is also required (to exclude hemorrhage), but the question specifically asks about laboratory checks.

stem: MCA stroke affecting the dominant hemisphere causes:

choices.feedback: Leg weakness predominates in ACA strokes, not MCA.

Correct! Dominant (usually left) MCA: Broca aphasia (frontal — understands but can't speak fluently) or Wernicke aphasia (temporal — fluent but nonsensical speech, impaired comprehension) + contralateral face and arm > leg weakness + homonymous hemianopia. Non-dominant MCA: hemispatial neglect.
Bilateral leg weakness suggests spinal cord pathology, not MCA stroke.

choices.text: Ipsilateral face + contralateral body sensory loss
choices.feedback: This crossed pattern = lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome from PICA occlusion.

explanation: The middle cerebral artery (MCA) supplies the lateral cerebral cortex. MCA syndrome is understood through the cortical homunculus: the lateral cortex corresponds to face and upper extremity representation (leg is supplied by ACA on the medial surface). Dominant hemisphere (usually left) MCA stroke produces aphasia (Broca's if frontal/expressive, Wernicke's if temporal/receptive, or global if both) plus contralateral face and arm weakness with relative leg sparing. Non-dominant (usually right) MCA stroke produces hemispatial neglect, anosognosia, and contralateral face/arm weakness without aphasia. The MCA is the most commonly affected artery in ischemic stroke.

stem: Thunderclap headache — 'worst headache of my life.' First test?

choices.feedback: CT must be done BEFORE LP to rule out mass lesion and to look for blood. LP is second-line if CT negative.
Evaluates carotid stenosis, not SAH.
MRI is not first-line for acute SAH — too slow and less sensitive for acute blood.

Correct! CT is ~95-98% sensitive within 6h for SAH (hyperdense blood in subarachnoid space/cisterns). If CT negative but clinical suspicion remains → lumbar puncture (xanthochromia = bilirubin from RBC breakdown, confirms SAH). CT → LP → CTA to find aneurysm.

explanation: A thunderclap headache ('worst headache of my life') with maximal intensity at onset is the classic presentation of subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH) until proven otherwise. Non-contrast CT head is the first test — it has very high sensitivity for SAH within the first 6-12 hours (approaching 98-100% with modern scanners). If CT is negative but clinical suspicion remains high, lumbar puncture is performed to look for xanthochromia (yellow discoloration from bilirubin in CSF, indicating prior bleeding). CT angiography (CTA) is used to identify the source aneurysm. MRI is not the first-line acute test. The most common cause of non-traumatic SAH is rupture of a saccular (berry) aneurysm, most frequently at the anterior communicating artery.

stem: SAH vasospasm peaks at what time after hemorrhage?

choices.feedback: Rebleeding risk is highest in first 24h, but vasospasm peaks later.

Correct! Vasospasm peaks days 4-14 post-SAH. Presents as new/worsening neurologic deficit. Nimodipine (CCB) given for 21 days — improves outcomes (neuroprotective). Treatment of symptomatic vasospasm: triple-H therapy + intra-arterial vasodilators. Monitor with transcranial Doppler.
By day 21, vasospasm risk has largely passed.
Vasospasm is an acute/subacute complication, not chronic.

explanation: Cerebral vasospasm is the most feared delayed complication of subarachnoid hemorrhage, peaking between days 4-14 after the initial bleed (most common around days 7-10). Vasospasm causes delayed cerebral ischemia (DCI), which is a major contributor to morbidity and mortality in SAH survivors. The mechanism involves breakdown products of subarachnoid blood (particularly oxyhemoglobin) causing sustained arterial smooth muscle contraction. Nimodipine (a calcium channel blocker) is the standard prophylactic treatment — it improves neurological outcomes, likely through neuroprotection rather than direct vasodilation. Transcranial Doppler ultrasonography monitors for vasospasm by detecting increased flow velocities in cerebral arteries.

stem: Permissive hypertension in acute ischemic stroke means:

choices.feedback: Correct! In acute ischemic stroke, elevated BP helps maintain perfusion to the ischemic penumbra. Do not treat unless >220/120 (non-tPA candidates) or >185/110 (tPA candidates — must lower before giving tPA). Labetalol or nicardipine for controlled lowering.

choices.text: BP management is irrelevant in stroke
choices.feedback: BP management is critical but the approach differs between ischemic and hemorrhagic stroke.

choices.text: Aggressively lower BP to normal immediately
choices.feedback: Aggressive lowering can worsen ischemia in the penumbra.

choices.text: Keep SBP above 200 at all times
choices.feedback: The goal is permissive, not actively maintaining extreme hypertension.

explanation: Permissive hypertension in acute ischemic stroke means allowing blood pressure to remain elevated to maintain perfusion to the ischemic penumbra (the at-risk tissue surrounding the infarct core). In patients NOT receiving tPA, blood pressure should not be treated unless it exceeds 220/120 mmHg. In patients receiving tPA, the threshold is lower — blood pressure must be maintained below 185/110 before and during tPA administration, and below 180/105 for 24 hours afterward. Aggressive blood pressure lowering in acute ischemic stroke can worsen ischemia by reducing collateral perfusion to the penumbra. This is fundamentally different from hemorrhagic stroke, where blood pressure reduction is a primary treatment goal.

stem: Wallenberg syndrome (lateral medullary) results from occlusion of:

choices.feedback: Basilar occlusion causes locked-in syndrome or extensive brainstem signs.

Correct! PICA occlusion → lateral medullary (Wallenberg) syndrome. Classic findings: ipsilateral face + contralateral body pain/temperature loss (crossed sensory), dysphagia, hoarseness, vertigo/nystagmus, ipsilateral Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis), ipsilateral cerebellar ataxia. No motor weakness (corticospinal tract spared).
ACA causes contralateral leg weakness.
MCA causes contralateral face + arm weakness and aphasia/neglect.

explanation: Wallenberg syndrome (lateral medullary syndrome) results from occlusion of the posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) or its parent vessel, the vertebral artery. The classic presentation includes ipsilateral facial pain/numbness (descending trigeminal tract), ipsilateral Horner syndrome (descending sympathetic fibers), ipsilateral cerebellar ataxia (inferior cerebellar peduncle), ipsilateral palatal/vocal cord paralysis with dysphagia/hoarseness (nucleus ambiguus), and contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation in the body (spinothalamic tract). Notably, there is NO limb weakness (corticospinal tract is spared as it runs medially). The crossed sensory pattern (ipsilateral face, contralateral body) is the hallmark distinguishing feature.

stem: A patient is having continuous generalized tonic-clonic seizures for 15 minutes despite IV lorazepam. What is the diagnosis and next treatment step?

choices.text: Status epilepticus — IV fosphenytoin or levetiracetam as second-line
choices.feedback: Correct! Status epilepticus: continuous seizure >5 minutes or ≥2 seizures without return to baseline. Life-threatening emergency. Treatment ladder: (1) Benzodiazepines first-line (lorazepam, may repeat once; or midazolam IM if no IV). (2) If BZD fails: fosphenytoinPE/kg IV, OR levetiracetam, OR valproate. (3) Refractory: continuous infusion midazolam, propofol, or pentobarbital + continuous EEG monitoring + ICU. Also: ABCs, glucose check, thiamine if alcoholism suspected.

choices.text: Complex partial seizure — increase oral antiepileptic
choices.feedback: 15 minutes of continuous generalized tonic-clonic seizures is status epilepticus requiring emergent IV treatment, not outpatient medication adjustment.
Febrile seizures are brief (<5 min) and self-limited. Continuous seizures for 15 minutes is status epilepticus.

choices.text: Psychogenic non-epileptic event — no pharmacologic treatment needed
choices.feedback: PNEE is a consideration but 15-minute continuous generalized seizures must be treated as status epilepticus until proven otherwise. Safety first.

explanation: Status epilepticus is defined as continuous seizure activity lasting >5 minutes or ≥2 seizures without return to baseline consciousness. It is a neurological emergency with mortality of 10-20%. First-line treatment is IV benzodiazepines (lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg or midazolam IM if no IV access). If seizures persist after adequate benzodiazepine dosing, second-line agents include fosphenytoin (preferred over phenytoin for better safety profile), levetiracetam, or valproic acid. If seizures continue (refractory status epilepticus), continuous IV infusion of propofol, midazolam, or pentobarbital with continuous EEG monitoring is required.

stem: Which antiepileptic drug is the most common cause of teratogenic neural tube defects?

choices.feedback: Carbamazepine has some teratogenicity risk (~3-5%) including neural tube defects, but the risk is significantly lower than valproate.

Correct! Valproate has the highest teratogenicity risk among AEDs: 10-15% major malformation rate (vs 2-3% general population). Neural tube defects (spina bifida) are the hallmark — 1-2% risk. Also: cardiac defects, cleft palate, cognitive/behavioral effects (decreased IQ in exposed children). Contraindicated in pregnancy (category X). All women on valproate must use effective contraception. High-dose folic acid partially reduces but does NOT eliminate neural tube defect risk. Safer alternatives: levetiracetam, lamotrigine.
Gabapentin has limited data but no established major teratogenic risk comparable to valproate.
Levetiracetam has the best safety profile in pregnancy among AEDs. It is preferred in women of childbearing age.

explanation: Valproic acid is the most teratogenic commonly used antiepileptic drug. It carries a significant risk of neural tube defects (spina bifida, ~1-2% risk), as well as cardiac malformations, craniofacial abnormalities, and neurodevelopmental delays (lower IQ, increased autism risk). It is classified as pregnancy category X by many guidelines. Women of childbearing potential on valproate should receive high-dose folic acid supplementation and counseling about contraception. If possible, valproate should be switched to a safer alternative (levetiracetam, lamotrigine) before conception. Carbamazepine and phenytoin also carry teratogenic risks but generally less than valproate.

It is classified as pregnancy category X by many guidelines. Women of childbearing potential on valproate should receive high-dose folic acid supplementation and counseling about contraception. If possible, valproate should be switched to a safer alternative (levetiracetam, lamotrigine) before conception. Carbamazepine and phenytoin also carry teratogenic risks but generally less than valproate.

stem: What first-line medication is recommended for absence seizures?

choices.feedback: Phenobarbital is not effective for absence seizures. It is used for neonatal seizures and as a last-line agent for other seizure types.
Phenytoin is effective for focal and generalized tonic-clonic seizures but is INEFFECTIVE for absence seizures and may worsen them.
Topiramate has broad-spectrum activity but ethosuximide remains first-line specifically for absence seizures.

Correct! Ethosuximide: first-line for PURE absence seizures (childhood absence epilepsy). Mechanism: blocks T-type calcium channels in thalamic neurons → inhibits the thalamocortical oscillation circuit that generates 3 Hz spike-and-wave absence seizures. If mixed seizure types (absence + generalized tonic-clonic): valproate or lamotrigine (ethosuximide only covers absence). Avoid: carbamazepine, phenytoin, gabapentin, tiagabine → can WORSEN absence seizures.

explanation: Ethosuximide is the first-line treatment for absence seizures (childhood absence epilepsy). It works by blocking T-type calcium channels in thalamic relay neurons, which are responsible for generating the 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges characteristic of absence seizures. Absence seizures present as brief (5-30 seconds) episodes of staring with behavioral arrest, often with eyelid fluttering or subtle automatisms, and abrupt return to baseline. EEG shows the pathognomonic 3 Hz generalized spike-and-wave pattern. Valproic acid is an alternative (especially if there are concurrent generalized tonic-clonic seizures), but ethosuximide is preferred for pure absence epilepsy due to fewer side effects.
