stem: A patient with severe TBI has a GCS of 7 and ICP monitor showing 28 mmHg (normal < 20). What is the initial management?

choices.feedback: Corticosteroids are NOT recommended for TBI-associated cerebral edema (CRASH trial showed increased mortality). Steroids help with vasogenic edema from tumors, not TBI.
LP is CONTRAINDICATED with elevated ICP and mass effect — risk of transtentorial herniation. Ventricular drainage (EVD) is used instead.
Surgery is reserved for refractory ICP elevation after medical management fails. Start with medical interventions.

choices.text: Elevate head of bed to 30°, IV mannitol or hypertonic saline, hyperventilation as temporizing measure
choices.feedback: Correct! Elevated ICP management ladder: (1) Head of bed 30° (optimize venous drainage), midline head position, loosen cervical collar. (2) Sedation/analgesia (propofol, fentanyl — reduce metabolic demand). (3) Osmotic therapy: mannitol 20% OR hypertonic saline 23.4% (30 mL via central line). (4) Brief hyperventilation (target pCO2 30-35 mmHg — temporizing only, cerebral vasoconstriction). (5) CSF drainage via EVD. (6) Refractory: pentobarbital coma, decompressive craniectomy. Target: ICP < 22 mmHg, CPP 60-70 mmHg (CPP = MAP − ICP).

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Elevate head of bed to 30°, IV mannitol or hypertonic saline, hyperventilation as temporizing measure) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: A patient presents with bilateral loss of pain/temperature sensation below T10, preserved proprioception and vibration, and urinary retention. MRI shows an anterior spinal artery occlusion. What syndrome does this represent?

choices.feedback: Central cord syndrome (typically from hyperextension in cervical spondylosis) shows upper extremity > lower extremity weakness with variable sensory loss. It doesn't present as a clean sensory dissociation with T10 level.
Brown-Séquard (hemisection) causes ipsilateral motor loss + proprioception loss, with CONTRALATERAL pain/temperature loss. This patient has BILATERAL deficits, not unilateral.

Anterior spinal artery occlusion → infarction of anterior 2/3 of cord: bilateral corticospinal tracts (motor), spinothalamic tracts (pain/temperature), and autonomic tracts (bladder). SPARES posterior columns (proprioception, vibration, fine touch). Worst prognosis of incomplete cord syndromes. Causes: aortic surgery, dissection, atherosclerosis, hypotension.
Posterior cord syndrome (rare) affects ONLY posterior columns → loss of proprioception and vibration with preserved motor and pain/temperature. This is the opposite of what's described.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Anterior cord syndrome) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.
verifiedExplanation: Bilateral loss of pain and temperature sensation with preserved proprioception and vibration sense below a spinal level indicates anterior cord syndrome. The anterior spinal artery supplies the anterior two-thirds of the cord (spinothalamic tracts for pain/temp, corticospinal tracts for motor). The posterior columns (proprioception, vibration, fine touch) are preserved because they receive blood supply from the posterior spinal arteries. Common causes include aortic surgery, aortic dissection, and anterior spinal artery thrombosis.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: anterior cord syndrome with bilateral pain/temperature loss preserving posterior column function

stem: A 70-year-old falls and develops weakness in all four extremities, worse in the arms than legs, with cape-like sensory loss. MRI shows cervical cord hemorrhage. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Central cord syndrome: most common incomplete cord injury. Hyperextension in elderly with cervical spondylosis → central cord hemorrhage/edema. Arms weaker than legs (because upper extremity tracts are more medial/central in corticospinal tract arrangement). 'Cape-like' sensory loss at level of injury (crossing spinothalamic fibers in anterior commissure). Often preserves bladder and lower extremity function. Best prognosis of incomplete cord syndromes.
Cauda equina involves peripheral nerve roots below L1-L2 level → lower motor neuron pattern with saddle anesthesia. This patient has cervical pathology with upper extremity predominant weakness.

Anterior cord syndrome would show bilateral motor and pain/temperature loss BELOW the lesion with preserved posterior column function. The arm > leg pattern and cape distribution point to central cord syndrome.

Complete transection would cause total loss of all motor/sensory function below the level. The preserved lower extremity function and specific arm > leg pattern indicate an incomplete (central) injury.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Central cord syndrome) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.
verifiedExplanation: Central cord syndrome classically presents with upper extremity weakness greater than lower extremity weakness, with variable sensory loss and bladder dysfunction. It is the most common incomplete spinal cord injury, typically seen in older patients with pre-existing cervical spondylosis who sustain a hyperextension injury. The central cord region contains the cervical motor tracts (arms are medially located in the corticospinal tract), explaining the disproportionate arm weakness. Management is generally conservative with supportive care.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: central cord syndrome with upper > lower extremity weakness

stem: A patient after a motorcycle accident has ipsilateral motor paralysis and proprioception loss with contralateral pain and temperature loss below T8. What cord syndrome is this?

choices.feedback: Brown-Séquard: hemisection damages ipsilateral corticospinal tract (motor), ipsilateral dorsal columns (proprioception/vibration), and contralateral spinothalamic tract (pain/temperature — fibers cross 1-2 levels above entry). Causes: penetrating trauma, tumors, MS. Best prognosis of all cord syndromes — >90% regain ambulatory function.
Posterior cord syndrome would only affect proprioception/vibration bilaterally. It wouldn't explain the motor paralysis or contralateral pain/temperature loss.
Conus medullaris affects the sacral cord (S2-S4) → saddle anesthesia, symmetric lower extremity findings, and early bladder/bowel dysfunction. This thoracic-level hemisection is Brown-Séquard.

Anterior cord syndrome is BILATERAL (both sides affected) with motor and pain/temperature loss, sparing proprioception. The unilateral motor deficit with crossed sensory loss here is classic hemisection.

explanation: Correct: Brown-Séquard syndrome (cord hemisection).
verifiedExplanation: Brown-Séquard syndrome (hemisection of the cord) presents with ipsilateral motor paralysis and loss of proprioception/vibration (corticospinal tract and posterior columns on the same side), plus contralateral loss of pain and temperature sensation (spinothalamic tract crosses at the anterior white commissure 1-2 levels above entry). Most commonly caused by penetrating trauma. It has the best prognosis of the incomplete cord syndromes, with >90% of patients regaining ambulatory function.

stem: A patient with sudden-onset back pain and bilateral lower extremity weakness is found to have cauda equina syndrome on MRI. What is the most urgent next step?

choices.feedback: High-dose steroids were historically used for spinal cord injury (NASCIS protocol) but are no longer standard of care due to side effects and minimal benefit. CES requires surgical decompression, not steroids.

Cauda equina syndrome (CES) is a surgical emergency. Compression of lumbosacral nerve roots → bilateral leg weakness, saddle anesthesia (S2-S4), urinary retention (overflow incontinence), decreased rectal tone. Most common cause: large central disc herniation at L4-L5 or L5-S1. Delay >48 hours significantly worsens outcomes for bladder and bowel function. MRI confirms diagnosis; surgery should not be delayed.

choices.text: Lumbar puncture to rule out infection
choices.feedback: LP is not indicated and would be dangerous if there's a mass lesion causing compression. MRI has already confirmed the diagnosis. Proceed to surgical decompression.

choices.text: Conservative management with bed rest and NSAIDs for 6 weeks
choices.feedback: CES is a surgical emergency. Conservative management is appropriate for simple disc herniation without CES, but the presence of bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, and bilateral weakness mandates urgent surgery.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Emergent surgical decompression within 24-48 hours) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.
verifiedExplanation: Acute spinal cord compression (from epidural abscess, metastatic disease, or hematoma) with bilateral lower extremity weakness is a surgical emergency requiring decompression within 24-48 hours. The earlier the intervention, the better the neurologic outcome. MRI of the entire spine is the imaging modality of choice. High-dose IV corticosteroids (dexamethasone) should be started immediately if malignant compression is suspected. Cauda equina syndrome (saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, bilateral leg weakness) has a similar urgency.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: emergent decompression within 24-48 hours for acute cord compression

stem: A patient with vitamin B12 deficiency develops progressive gait ataxia and bilateral loss of vibration and proprioception in the lower extremities. Which spinal cord tracts are affected?

choices.feedback: Subacute combined degeneration (B12 deficiency) affects posterior columns → proprioception/vibration loss → sensory ataxia (positive Romberg). Also affects lateral corticospinal tracts → upper motor neuron signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski). Mechanism: B12 required for myelin synthesis (methylmalonyl-CoA pathway). Can be irreversible if untreated. Peripheral neuropathy often coexists.

Spinocerebellar tract disease (e.g., Friedreich ataxia) causes cerebellar-type ataxia. The sensory ataxia in B12 deficiency (positive Romberg, worse with eyes closed) is from posterior column dysfunction, not cerebellar pathways.

Anterior horn cell disease (e.g., ALS, polio) causes lower motor neuron signs — flaccidity, atrophy, fasciculations. B12 deficiency causes upper motor neuron signs from corticospinal tract demyelination.

Spinothalamic tracts carry pain and temperature sensation. B12 deficiency characteristically affects posterior columns (proprioception/vibration) and lateral corticospinal tracts (motor), not spinothalamic.

explanation: Correct: Posterior columns (dorsal columns — fasciculus gracilis and fasciculus cuneatus).
verifiedExplanation: Vitamin B12 deficiency causes subacute combined degeneration, which primarily affects the posterior columns (proprioception, vibration, fine touch) and lateral corticospinal tracts (motor). The posterior column involvement explains the gait ataxia (sensory ataxia from impaired proprioception) and loss of vibration sense. Positive Romberg sign is characteristic. B12 is essential for myelin synthesis; deficiency causes demyelination. Other findings include peripheral neuropathy, megaloblastic anemia, and neuropsychiatric symptoms.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: posterior column degeneration in B12 deficiency causing gait ataxia

stem: A patient with traumatic brain injury develops unilateral pupil dilation, contralateral hemiparesis, and decreasing consciousness. What is the most likely cause?

choices.text: Uncal herniation from expanding epidural or subdural hematoma
choices.feedback: Uncal herniation: expanding mass lesion pushes temporal lobe medially → compresses CN III (ipsilateral 'blown' pupil — parasympathetic fibers on outside of nerve) → compresses cerebral peduncle (contralateral hemiparesis) → compresses brainstem (decreasing consciousness). Cushing's triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular breathing) is a late sign of increased ICP. Emergent CT → surgical evacuation.
Basilar skull fracture presents with Battle sign (mastoid ecchymosis), raccoon eyes, CSF rhinorrhea/otorrhea. It doesn't cause the classic herniation syndrome described here.
Vasospasm is associated with subarachnoid hemorrhage (typically days 3-14), not acute TBI. It causes focal neurologic deficits from ischemia but not the classic herniation pattern.

DAI causes widespread microscopic axonal damage → immediate prolonged unconsciousness, typically without focal signs like unilateral pupil dilation. It's from rotational acceleration/deceleration forces.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Uncal herniation from expanding epidural or subdural hematoma) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: Which initial intervention is most appropriate for acute management of elevated ICP in a traumatic brain injury patient?

choices.feedback: While historically done in extremis, current standard requires CT first to localize pathology. Blind burr holes risk wrong location and delay definitive treatment.

choices.text: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with isotonic crystalloid
choices.feedback: While maintaining adequate CPP is important (avoid hypotension), aggressive fluid loading without ICP management can worsen cerebral edema. The priority is reducing ICP with osmotic therapy and positional measures.

CRASH trial definitively showed corticosteroids INCREASE mortality in TBI. They are contraindicated. This is different from their use in vasogenic edema from tumors (where dexamethasone is beneficial).

choices.text: Head of bed elevation to 30° and IV mannitol or hypertonic saline
choices.feedback: Stepwise ICP management: (1) HOB 30° (promotes venous drainage), head midline (avoid jugular compression), (2) osmotic therapy — mannitolor 23.4% hypertonic saline (creates osmotic gradient drawing water out of brain), (3) brief hyperventilation (target pCO2 30-35 for emergency herniation only — prolonged hyperventilation worsens ischemia), (4) sedation/paralysis if needed, (5) surgical decompression if refractory. Target ICP <22 mmHg, CPP >60 mmHg.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Head of bed elevation to 30° and IV mannitol or hypertonic saline) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: A CT scan of a patient with head trauma shows a biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdense lesion in the temporal region. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: SAH fills cisterns and sulci with blood on CT — appears as diffuse hyperdensity in subarachnoid space, not a focal convex collection.

Contusions are intraparenchymal hemorrhagic lesions, typically in inferior frontal and anterior temporal lobes (coup-contrecoup). They appear as irregular hemorrhagic areas within brain tissue, not extra-axial collections.

choices.text: Subdural hematoma from bridging vein rupture
choices.feedback: Subdural hematomas are CRESCENT-shaped (concave), not biconvex. They form between dura and arachnoid from torn bridging veins. Cross suture lines. More common in elderly and anticoagulated patients.

choices.text: Epidural hematoma from middle meningeal artery rupture
choices.feedback: Epidural hematoma: biconvex/lens-shaped because blood is between skull and dura (dura is adherent to skull, limiting spread). Most common cause: temporal bone fracture → rupture of middle meningeal artery. Classic 'lucid interval': brief LOC → improvement → rapid deterioration from expanding hematoma. SURGICAL EMERGENCY: craniotomy for evacuation. Doesn't cross suture lines (dura adheres at sutures).

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Epidural hematoma from middle meningeal artery rupture) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. Key principles include anatomical localization of deficits, time course of symptom onset (acute vs subacute vs chronic), and evidence-based management algorithms.

stem: What does the Cushing reflex (hypertension with reflex bradycardia) indicate in a patient with head trauma?

choices.text: Acute myocardial infarction from cardiac contusion
choices.feedback: Cardiac contusion causes arrhythmias and potentially hypotension, not the specific pattern of hypertension with bradycardia that characterizes the Cushing reflex.
Pain causes tachycardia and hypertension (sympathetic activation), not bradycardia. The reflex bradycardia in Cushing's triad specifically indicates brainstem compression.

choices.text: Dangerously elevated intracranial pressure with impending brainstem herniation
choices.feedback: Cushing's triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) is a late, ominous sign of critically elevated ICP. Mechanism: rising ICP → brainstem ischemia → sympathetic surge (hypertension) → baroreceptor reflex (bradycardia) → respiratory center dysfunction. This is a pre-terminal event requiring IMMEDIATE intervention (osmotic therapy, potential surgery). CPP = MAP - ICP; the body raises MAP to maintain perfusion.
Spinal shock causes hypotension with bradycardia (loss of sympathetic tone below the lesion). The HYPERTENSION in Cushing's triad distinguishes it from spinal shock.

explanation: This question tests neurological clinical reasoning. The correct answer (Dangerously elevated intracranial pressure with impending brainstem herniation) is supported by current neurology guidelines and StatPearls evidence. stem: A 22-year-old has recurrent episodes of staring, lip smacking, and fumbling hand movements lasting 60-90 seconds, followed by confusion. EEG shows temporal lobe spikes. What type of seizure is this?

choices.feedback: Absence seizures show brief (<20 sec) staring spells with abrupt onset/offset and NO postictal confusion. EEG shows generalized 3-Hz spike-and-wave, not temporal spikes. Typically begin in childhood.
Myoclonic seizures are brief, shock-like jerks without altered consciousness. They don't cause the prolonged altered awareness and automatisms described.

choices.text: Focal seizure with impaired awareness (complex partial seizure)
choices.feedback: Focal seizure with impaired awareness (formerly complex partial): arises from temporal lobe → automatisms (lip smacking, chewing, hand fumbling), altered consciousness, postictal confusion. Temporal lobe epilepsy is the most common focal epilepsy in adults. First-line: carbamazepine, lamotrigine, or levetiracetam. May be amenable to surgical resection (anterior temporal lobectomy) if medically refractory.
GTC seizures involve bilateral tonic stiffening followed by clonic jerking of all extremities, not the focal automatisms and staring described here.

explanation: Staring, lip smacking, and hand automatisms with impaired awareness is the classic presentation of a focal seizure with impaired awareness (previously called complex partial seizure). These seizures most commonly originate in the temporal lobe, which explains the automatisms (oroalimentary like lip smacking, chewing, and manual like picking, fumbling). The patient cannot respond appropriately during the event and typically has no memory of it afterward. Post-ictal confusion is common. Temporal lobe epilepsy is the most common form of focal epilepsy in adults. First-line treatment includes carbamazepine, lamotrigine, or levetiracetam.

stem: A patient on seizure prophylaxis develops Stevens-Johnson syndrome. Which antiepileptic is most commonly associated with this reaction, particularly in patients of Asian descent with HLA-B*1502?

choices.feedback: Valproate's major concerns are hepatotoxicity, pancreatitis, teratogenicity, and thrombocytopenia — not SJS. It's not among the high-risk AEDs for hypersensitivity reactions.

Carbamazepine carries the highest risk of SJS/TEN, especially in patients of Southeast Asian descent with HLA-B*1502 allele. Guidelines recommend HLA-B*1502 testing before starting carbamazepine in at-risk populations. Other AEDs with SJS risk: phenytoin, lamotrigine (especially with rapid titration or concurrent valproate), phenobarbital. Lamotrigine risk is minimized by slow titration.
Gabapentin has a mild side effect profile (dizziness, sedation) and is not associated with significant SJS risk.

Levetiracetam has a very favorable side effect profile with minimal risk of SJS. Main side effects: irritability, behavioral changes, somnolence. It's often preferred partly because of its safety profile.

explanation: Carbamazepine is the antiepileptic drug most commonly associated with Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS) and toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN). The risk is strongly associated with the HLA-B*1502 allele, which is prevalent in Southeast Asian populations — Guidelines recommend screening for this allele before starting carbamazepine in at-risk populations. Other AEDs associated with SJS/TEN include phenytoin, lamotrigine (especially with rapid dose escalation), and phenobarbital. SJS presents with mucocutaneous lesions, targetoid rash, and skin detachment (<10% BSA for SJS, >30% for TEN). It requires immediate drug discontinuation and supportive care, often in a burn unit.

stem: A 30-year-old man presents after a motorcycle accident with loss of pain and temperature sensation on the right body below T10, loss of proprioception and vibration on the left below T10, and left leg weakness with upper motor neuron signs. What is the diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct: Brown-Séquard syndrome (spinal cord hemisection). Brown-Séquard syndrome results from hemisection of the spinal cord. Key features: ipsilateral motor loss (corticospinal tract) and ipsilateral proprioception/vibration loss (dorsal columns) with contralateral pain/temperature loss (spinothalamic tract—which crosses 1-2 levels above entry). In this case, the LEFT cord is injured at T10: left motor loss, left dorsal column loss, and RIGHT spinothalamic loss (pain/temp crosses, so contralateral loss). Brown-Séquard has the best prognosis among incomplete cord syndromes, with >90% regaining ambulatory function.

Reference: Pulmonary Embolism - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560551/ (StatPearls, 2025)

stem: A 70-year-old man undergoes aortic aneurysm repair and awakens with bilateral leg paralysis, loss of pain and temperature sensation below T8, absent lower extremity reflexes, and urinary retention. However, proprioception and vibration sense are intact in both legs. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Anterior cord syndrome from anterior spinal artery occlusion

explanation:
Anterior spinal artery syndrome classically occurs after aortic surgery (artery of Adamkiewicz arises from the aorta at T9-T12). It damages the anterior two-thirds of the cord: bilateral motor loss (corticospinal tracts), bilateral pain/temperature loss (spinothalamic tracts), and autonomic dysfunction (urinary retention). Posterior columns are spared (supplied by posterior spinal arteries), preserving proprioception and vibration. This has the worst prognosis among incomplete cord syndromes. The initial areflexia represents spinal shock.

Reference: Neonatal RDS - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK563110/ (StatPearls, 2025)

stem: A 60-year-old man with cervical spondylosis falls and hyperextends his neck. He presents with upper extremity weakness greater than lower extremity weakness, with 'cape-like' sensory loss to pain and temperature across the shoulders. Bowel and bladder function are relatively preserved. What syndrome does this represent?
explanation:
Central cord syndrome is the most common incomplete spinal cord injury, typically occurring in elderly patients with pre-existing cervical spondylosis after hyperextension injuries (falls). The central cord contains the upper extremity motor fibers (somatotopic organization: arms medial, legs lateral in the corticospinal tract), so central damage preferentially affects arms > legs. The 'cape-like' sensory loss reflects damage to crossing spinothalamic fibers at the level of injury. Lower extremity and sacral functions (including bladder) are relatively spared because their fibers run more peripherally.

Reference: Pneumonia - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK525774/ (StatPearls, 2025)

stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with progressive bilateral leg stiffness, difficulty walking for 6 months, hyperreflexia in the lower extremities, sustained ankle clonus, and bilateral Babinski signs. Sensory exam and upper extremities are normal. MRI shows T2 hyperintensity in the lateral columns of the thoracic spinal cord. What is the most likely etiology?

choices.text: Compressive myelopathy from thoracic disc herniation
Primary lateral sclerosis or hereditary spastic paraplegia

explanation: Progressive bilateral UMN signs (spastic paraparesis) with lateral column involvement on MRI and NO sensory findings points to a pure motor tract disorder—primary lateral sclerosis or hereditary spastic paraplegia. MS typically has relapsing-remitting course with both motor and sensory findings plus periventricular/juxtacortical lesions. B12 deficiency affects dorsal columns (proprioception, vibration) and lateral corticospinal tracts (subacute combined degeneration) but includes sensory ataxia. Compressive myelopathy usually has a sensory level. The selective lateral column involvement is the key distinguishing feature.
verifiedExplanation: Progressive bilateral leg stiffness with spasticity, hyperreflexia, and extensor plantar responses (upgoing toes) without sensory loss suggests a pure upper motor neuron process affecting the lateral corticospinal tracts. The differential includes primary lateral sclerosis (a variant of ALS affecting only UMN), hereditary spastic paraplegia, and spinal cord compression. The absence of sensory findings helps distinguish this from cord compression or B12 deficiency. EMG/NCS and MRI are essential for workup.

stem: A 50-year-old man presents with back pain, rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness, urinary retention, and saddle anesthesia developing over 24 hours. MRI shows a mass compressing the conus medullaris at L1. What is the most time-sensitive intervention?

choices.text: Urgent lumbar puncture with CSF cytology
Start empiric antibiotics for epidural abscess
IV dexamethasone followed by emergent surgical decompression or radiation
MRI of the entire spine before any treatment

explanation: Conus medullaris syndrome from cord compression is a neurosurgical emergency. The conus (L1-L2) contains sacral segments controlling bowel, bladder, and sexual function. Once these neurons are damaged, recovery is poor. High-dose IV dexamethasone (bolus) reduces vasogenic edema and should be given immediately while arranging definitive treatment (surgery for single-level disease, radiation for radiosensitive tumors or multilevel disease). Neurological outcome correlates with pre-treatment function and speed of intervention. Urinary retention with saddle anesthesia indicates the window for recovery is closing rapidly.
verifiedExplanation: This presentation — rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness with back pain — suggests malignant spinal cord compression until proven otherwise. Immediate management is IV dexamethasone (to reduce tumor-related edema) followed by emergent MRI and surgical decompression or radiation. Metastatic epidural compression most commonly involves the thoracic spine. Delay in treatment beyond 24-48 hours after onset of motor deficits dramatically worsens the chance of neurologic recovery. Prostate, breast, and lung cancers are the most common primary sites.

verifiedReferences.supportingQuote: IV steroids and emergent decompression for malignant cord compression

stem: The characteristic CSF finding in Guillain-Barré syndrome is:

choices.feedback: This pattern suggests bacterial meningitis, not GBS.

choices.text: Albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein, normal cell count)
choices.feedback: Correct! GBS CSF: elevated protein (>0.45 g/L) with normal WBC count (<10 cells/μL). Protein may be normal in the first week. If WBC >50, consider HIV-associated polyradiculopathy or CMV. Nerve conduction studies: demyelinating pattern (AIDP most common) or axonal pattern (AMAN, AMSAN). Anti-ganglioside antibodies: anti-GQ1b in Miller Fisher variant.
This suggests subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Oligoclonal bands are characteristic of multiple sclerosis.

GBS CSF: albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein, normal WBC). May be normal in first week. WBC >50 → consider HIV/CMV. NCS: demyelinating (AIDP) or axonal (AMAN).

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with optic neuritis followed weeks later by leg weakness. MRI shows periventricular white matter lesions. The diagnostic criteria require:

choices.text: Dissemination in space (multiple CNS areas) and time (different episodes)
choices.feedback: Correct! McDonald criteria for MS: lesions disseminated in space (≥2 of: periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord) AND time (≥2 attacks, or new lesions on follow-up MRI, or simultaneous enhancing + non-enhancing lesions). CSF oligoclonal bands can substitute for DIT.

choices.text: A single demyelinating event with positive ANA
choices.feedback: ANA is not part of MS diagnosis. A single event is clinically isolated syndrome (CIS), not definitive MS.
Simultaneous bilateral optic neuritis is atypical for MS and should raise concern for neuromyelitis optica (NMO/NMOSD).
This describes primary progressive MS, which has different criteria and accounts for only ~10-15% of MS cases.

explanation:
Multiple sclerosis (MS) diagnosis requires demonstration of CNS demyelination disseminated in space (DIS) and time (DIT) per the McDonald criteria. DIS: lesions in ≥2 of 4 characteristic CNS areas (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord). DIT: ≥2 clinical attacks occurring at different times, OR new T2/enhancing lesions on follow-up MRI, OR simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on a single MRI. CSF oligoclonal bands (present in >95% of MS) can substitute for DIT. Most common form: relapsing-remitting MS (85%), characterized by discrete attacks followed by partial/complete recovery.

stem: First-line disease-modifying therapy for relapsing-remitting MS includes:

choices.text: Interferon-beta, glatiramer acetate, or newer oral agents (dimethyl fumarate, teriflunomide)
choices.feedback: Correct! Platform therapies (injectable: IFN-β, glatiramer; oral: dimethyl fumarate, teriflunomide, fingolimod) reduce relapse rate by ~30-50%. Higher-efficacy agents (natalizumab, ocrelizumab, ofatumumab) may be used first-line for aggressive disease.

explanation:
MS disease-modifying therapies (DMTs) aim to reduce relapse frequency, delay disability progression, and limit new MRI lesion accumulation. Platform (moderate-efficacy) therapies: interferon-beta preparations, glatiramer acetate, dimethyl fumarate, teriflunomide, fingolimod. Higher-efficacy therapies: natalizumab (anti-VLA-4, risk of PML), ocrelizumab (anti-CD20, also approved for primary progressive MS), ofatumumab, cladribine, alemtuzumab. Treatment of acute relapses: IV methylprednisolone 1g/day × 3-5 days. Plasma exchange for steroid-refractory relapses. The trend in MS management is toward early use of higher-efficacy therapies to prevent irreversible axonal damage.

stem: Uhthoff phenomenon in MS refers to:

choices.text: Worsening of neurological symptoms with increased body temperature (heat sensitivity)
choices.feedback: Correct! Elevated temperature (exercise, hot bath, fever) slows conduction in demyelinated axons → transient worsening of existing symptoms. Not a true relapse — symptoms resolve with cooling. Named after Wilhelm Uhthoff who described visual blurring after exercise.

choices.text: Permanent progression of disability between relapses
choices.feedback: This describes secondary progressive MS, not Uhthoff phenomenon.

choices.text: Electric shock sensation with neck flexion
choices.feedback: This is Lhermitte sign (suggests posterior column/cervical cord lesion), not Uhthoff phenomenon.

choices.text: Paradoxical improvement of symptoms in cold environments
choices.feedback: Cold environments may improve conduction in demyelinated fibers, but this is the opposite of Uhthoff phenomenon.

explanation:
Uhthoff phenomenon: transient worsening of MS symptoms with elevated body temperature. Mechanism: heat increases metabolic demand and further impairs saltatory conduction in demyelinated axons (already operating at reduced safety factor). Common triggers: hot baths, exercise, fever, saunas, warm weather. Most commonly affects vision (optic neuritis history) but can worsen any pre-existing deficit. Important clinical pearl: Uhthoff phenomenon is NOT a true relapse — it is a pseudo-exacerbation that resolves when temperature normalizes. No treatment change is needed. Other MS-associated signs: Lhermitte sign (electric shock sensation down spine with neck flexion — cervical cord demyelination), internuclear ophthalmoplegia (MLF lesion).

stem: The cardinal motor features of Parkinson disease include all EXCEPT:

choices.text: Intention tremor that worsens when reaching for objects

explanation:
Parkinson disease cardinal motor features (TRAP): Tremor (resting, 4-6 Hz, pill-rolling, asymmetric onset), Rigidity (cogwheel = lead-pipe rigidity + tremor), Akinesia/Bradykinesia (slowness and poverty of movement — required for diagnosis), Postural instability (late feature, leads to falls). Key distinction: PD tremor is a RESTING tremor that improves with intentional movement. Intention tremor (worsens approaching target) = cerebellar pathology. Essential tremor = action/postural tremor that improves at rest and worsens with action. PD pathology: loss of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra pars compacta, Lewy bodies (alpha-synuclein aggregates).

stem: First-line pharmacotherapy for motor symptoms in early Parkinson disease is:

choices.feedback: Correct! Levodopa (L-DOPA) is converted to dopamine in the brain. Combined with carbidopa (peripheral DOPA decarboxylase inhibitor) to prevent peripheral conversion and reduce nausea. Most effective symptomatic treatment at any stage.
Anticholinergics may help tremor in young patients but have significant cognitive side effects and are generally avoided in elderly patients.

choices.text: Deep brain stimulation as initial therapy
choices.feedback: DBS is reserved for patients with motor fluctuations refractory to medical therapy, not initial treatment.
COMT inhibitors (entacapone, tolcapone) are adjunctive to levodopa, not monotherapy.

explanation:
Carbidopa-levodopa is the gold standard treatment for PD motor symptoms. Levodopa crosses the BBB and is converted to dopamine by DOPA decarboxylase. Carbidopa inhibits peripheral decarboxylase → more levodopa reaches the brain, reduces peripheral side effects (nausea, hypotension). Alternatives for early/mild disease: dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole — risk of impulse control disorders), MAO-B inhibitors (selegiline, rasagiline — mild symptomatic benefit). Long-term levodopa complications: motor fluctuations (wearing-off, on-off phenomenon), dyskinesias (involuntary choreiform movements). Managed by dose adjustments, adding COMT inhibitors, MAO-B inhibitors, or eventually deep brain stimulation.

explanation:

explanation:
In spontaneous ICH, early aggressive BP reduction (target SBP <140 within 1 hour) is recommended to limit hematoma expansion, which is the primary driver of early deterioration. stem: Which location of intracerebral hemorrhage most commonly requires emergent surgical evacuation?

choices.text: Cerebellar hemorrhage >3 cm with brainstem compression or hydrocephalus
choices.feedback: Correct! Cerebellar hemorrhage is the one ICH location with clear surgical benefit. If >3 cm or causing brainstem compression/obstructive hydrocephalus → emergent posterior fossa decompression. Delay can lead to rapid herniation and death.
Supratentorial ICH surgery has not consistently shown benefit in trials (STICH, STICH II). Minimally invasive techniques are being studied.

explanation:
Spontaneous ICH surgical decision-making: Cerebellar hemorrhage — clearest indication for surgery. If >3 cm diameter OR causing brainstem compression OR obstructive hydrocephalus → emergent surgical evacuation. Patients can deteriorate rapidly from transtentorial or tonsillar herniation. Supratentorial ICH — surgical benefit is less clear. STICH and STICH II trials did not show consistent benefit for conventional craniotomy. MISTIE III (minimally invasive catheter-based evacuation) showed trend toward benefit for large hematomas. Current approach: consider surgery for lobar clots within 1 cm of surface in deteriorating patients. Deep hemorrhages (putamen, thalamus) and brainstem hemorrhages are generally managed medically.

stem: Empiric treatment for bacterial meningitis in an immunocompetent adult (18-50 years) is:

choices.text: Vancomycin + ceftriaxone + dexamethasone (given before or with first antibiotic dose)
choices.feedback: Correct! Vancomycin covers resistant S. pneumoniae, ceftriaxone covers N. meningitidis and susceptible pneumococcus. Dexamethasone reduces mortality and hearing loss in pneumococcal meningitis — must be given before or with first dose of antibiotics.

explanation:
Empiric bacterial meningitis treatment by age: Neonates (0-28 days): ampicillin + cefotaxime (or gentamicin). Infants/children (1 mo-18 yr): vancomycin + ceftriaxone. Chorea (involuntary, irregular, flowing movements) is a feature of Huntington disease, NOT Parkinson disease. Onset is typically asymmetric. Also: masked facies, micrographia, shuffling gait, decreased arm swing.

stem: First-line pharmacotherapy for motor symptoms of Parkinson disease is:

choices.feedback: Anticholinergics may help tremor in young patients but are limited by cognitive side effects, especially in elderly patients.

Correct! Levodopa (L-DOPA) is the most effective treatment for PD motor symptoms. It is converted to dopamine in the brain. Carbidopa inhibits peripheral DOPA decarboxylase → prevents peripheral conversion to dopamine → reduces nausea and allows more levodopa to cross the BBB. Long-term complications: wearing-off (end-of-dose deterioration), on-off fluctuations, and dyskinesias (involuntary choreiform movements from chronic dopaminergic stimulation). Dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) may be used as initial monotherapy in younger patients to delay levodopa-associated motor complications.
COMT inhibitors are always used as ADJUNCTS to levodopa (they extend its half-life), never as monotherapy.
DBS is reserved for patients with medication-refractory motor fluctuations or disabling dyskinesias despite optimized medical therapy.

stem: A patient develops ascending weakness 2 weeks after a diarrheal illness. Reflexes are absent. CSF shows elevated protein with normal cell count. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: GBS presents with ascending symmetric weakness, areflexia, and albuminocytologic dissociation in CSF (elevated protein, normal WBC count). It is an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy most commonly triggered by Campylobacter jejuni infection. Nerve conduction studies show demyelinating pattern. Autonomic dysfunction (BP lability, arrhythmias) is common. Peak severity by 2-4 weeks, then recovery. [StatPearls NBK532714]

stem: What is the treatment for Guillain-Barré syndrome?
explanation: Treatment: IVIG (0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days) OR plasmapheresis — they are equally effective but should NOT be combined (no added benefit, may reduce IVIG efficacy). stem: What is the most dangerous complication of GBS that requires ICU monitoring?
explanation: Respiratory failure occurs in ~30% of GBS patients due to ascending paralysis affecting the phrenic nerve and diaphragm. Serial FVC and NIF measurements are essential — intubate if FVC <20 mL/kg, NIF weaker than -30 cmH2O, or >30% decline. Autonomic dysfunction (arrhythmias, blood pressure instability) is another life-threatening complication requiring ICU monitoring. [StatPearls NBK532714]

explanation: Acute MS relapses are treated with high-dose IV methylprednisolone (1g/day for 3-5 days). This shortens relapse duration but does not alter long-term disability. stem: Which CSF finding supports the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis?

stem: A 68-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "My wife says my face is drooping and I can't move my right arm."Vital SignsBP 178/98HR 88RR 16Temp 98.6°FSpO₂ 97% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Acute Ischemic Stroke (Left MCA) is the most likely diagnosis. Sudden right-sided weakness and facial droop with aphasia. Classic left MCA territory.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Not activating stroke alert immediately - delays door-to-needle time
- Forgetting to check glucose STAT - hypoglycemia is a stroke mimic
- Missing tPA window by spending too long on history before imaging
- Lowering BP too aggressively - permissive hypertension maintains perfusion

Evidence: Ischemic Stroke - StatPearls - Hui C, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK499997. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Hemorrhagic Stroke: MUST rule out before tPA. CT is essential.
- Hypoglycemia: Stroke mimic! ALWAYS check glucose immediately.
- Todd's Paralysis: Post-seizure weakness. Would expect witnessed seizure.
- Complex Migraine: Can cause focal deficits but usually has headache.

Reference: Ischemic Stroke - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499997/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 55-year-old female presents to the clinic, stating: "The room is spinning when I move my head."Vital SignsBP 128/78HR 72RR 14Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

stem: A 67-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "My hand shakes and I'm moving slower."Vital SignsBP 128/78 sitting, 108/68 standingHR 68RR 14Temp 98.2°FSpO₂ 98% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Parkinson's Disease is the most likely diagnosis. Asymmetric rest tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, and shuffling gait in elderly male - classic PD presentation.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Assuming all tremor is essential tremor - must distinguish rest vs. action tremor
- Missing the bradykinesia and rigidity that distinguish PD from essential tremor

- Progressive Supranuclear Palsy: Would have early falls, vertical gaze palsy, axial rigidity more than limb.

stem: A 55-year-old female presents to the emergency department, stating: "Numbness in my hands and feet, and strange spots on my skin."Vital SignsBP 142/88HR 78RR 16Temp 98.6°FSpO₂ 98% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Chronic Arsenic Toxicity is the most likely diagnosis. Untested private well in Maine + neuropathy + Mees' lines + raindrop pigmentation + GI symptoms = classic chronic arsenic poisoning.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Not asking about water source - especially private wells
- Assuming municipal water is the source (it's regulated; private wells are not)
- Missing Mees' lines on nail examination
- Not recognizing 'raindrop' pigmentation pattern

- B12 Deficiency: Should check, but Mees' lines and skin changes aren't explained by B12 deficiency.
- Diabetic Neuropathy: No diabetes history; skin and nail changes suggest toxic etiology.

stem: A 65-year-old female presents to the emergency department, stating: "I had trouble speaking clearly earlier today, but it went away after about 20 minutes."Vital SignsBP 162/94HR 78RR 16Temp 98.1°FSpO₂ 98% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: TIA (Carotid stenosis) is the most likely diagnosis. Classic presentation: transient focal deficits (dysarthria, hemiparesis) that resolved, prior amaurosis fugax, carotid bruit, multiple vascular risk factors.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Assuming resolved symptoms mean no workup is needed - TIA is a warning sign of imminent stroke
- Missing the prior amaurosis fugax episode - this indicates carotid disease
- Not calculating ABCD2 score to risk stratify
- Not performing carotid imaging in patient with carotid bruit

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Stroke (CVA): Even though symptoms resolved, must rule out actual infarction with MRI. 30% of 'TIAs' <24h have DWI changes.
- Intracranial Hemorrhage: Hemorrhage can occasionally present with transient symptoms. CT needed.
- Seizure (Post-ictal): Seizure can cause transient focal deficits (Todd's paralysis), but no witnessed seizure activity.
- Migraine with Aura: Can cause transient neurological symptoms, but usually includes headache and visual aura.

stem: A 68-year-old male presents to the clinic, stating: "Sudden right-sided weakness and trouble speaking."Vital SignsBP 178/102HR 88RR 18Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 97% Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation:
Correct Answer: Acute Ischemic Stroke (MCA) is the most likely diagnosis. Sudden onset focal neuro deficits (hemiparesis, aphasia) + AFib + subtherapeutic INR = classic cardioembolic stroke.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Waiting for all labs before CT - only glucose and coags needed before tPA decision
- Treating blood pressure too aggressively - only treat if >185/110 for tPA, >220/120 otherwise
- Missing the history of dietary vitamin K increase reducing warfarin efficacy
- Not recognizing the prior 'clumsiness' as a TIA warning sign

Evidence: Atrial Fibrillation and Stroke - StatPearls - Witt DM, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK482256. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Hemorrhagic Stroke: Must rule out with CT BEFORE any thrombolytics. Can present similarly.
- Hypoglycemia: Can cause focal neurological deficits that mimic stroke. Check glucose immediately.
- TIA: Symptoms persist >60 min and NIHSS 18. By definition, this is now a stroke, not TIA.
- Seizure (postictal paralysis): Todd's paralysis can mimic stroke, but no seizure witnessed.

stem: A 38-year-old man presents with 5 days of progressive bilateral leg weakness now ascending to involve his arms. He had a Campylobacter gastroenteritis 2 weeks ago. His weakness is SYMMETRIC and ASCENDING. Deep tendon reflexes are ABSENT in all extremities. Sensation is intact. He reports difficulty taking a deep breath.Vital SignsBP 142/88HR 62RR 20SpO2 95%ExamMotor Bilateral LE 2/5, bilateral UE 3/5 (proximal = distal)Reflexes 0/4 throughout (areflexia)Sensation Intact to all modalitiesCranial nerves Bilateral facial weaknessRespiratory Forced vital capacity (FVC) 1.8 L (predicted 4.5 L) = 40% predictedNCS/EMG Prolonged distal latencies, reduced conduction velocities, conduction block = DEMYELINATING pattern What is the urgent treatment AND the critical monitoring parameter?

choices.text: High-dose IV corticosteroids — standard treatment for autoimmune neurologic disease
Plasma exchange only after respiratory failure develops
Observe without treatment since sensation is preserved

explanation:
Correct Answer: GBS: acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy. Post-infectious autoimmune attack on peripheral nerve myelin (molecular mimicry). Classic presentation: symmetric ascending weakness + areflexia starting 1-4 weeks after infection (Campylobacter #1, then CMV, EBV, influenza). Treatment: IVIG or plasmapheresis (NOT steroids — GBS is the major exception to steroids-for-autoimmune). Respiratory monitoring is life-saving: 30% require mechanical ventilation. 20/30/40 rule for intubation: FVC 30% from baseline. Autonomic dysfunction: affects 70%; labile BP (hypertension alternating with hypotension), bradycardia, tachycardia, urinary retention — can cause sudden cardiac death. CSF: albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein, normal cell count). NCS: demyelinating pattern. Nadir: 2-4 weeks, then gradual recovery over months.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Symmetric ascending weakness + areflexia = classic GBS pattern
- Campylobacter 2 weeks prior = classic trigger (molecular mimicry with GM1 gangliosides)
- Bilateral facial weakness = cranial nerve involvement (occurs in 50% of GBS)
- FVC 40% predicted = respiratory compromise requiring ICU monitoring
- Areflexia with intact sensation = motor-predominant neuropathy (typical GBS)
- Demyelinating pattern on NCS = AIDP (most common GBS variant)
- Treatment: IVIG or plasmapheresis, NOT steroids

Evidence: Guillain-Barre Syndrome - StatPearls - Donofrio PD, et al. StatPearls. NBK532254. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- IV corticosteroids: GBS is the MAJOR EXCEPTION to steroids in autoimmune neurologic disease. Multiple RCTs have shown steroids provide NO benefit and may be harmful in GBS. This is in stark contrast to MS relapses, myasthenia exacerbations, and CIDP, where steroids ARE effective. The pathophysiology (complement-mediated peripheral nerve demyelination) does not respond to steroids.
- Wait for respiratory failure: Waiting for respiratory failure to start treatment is dangerous. Both IVIG and plasmapheresis should be started AS SOON AS the diagnosis is suspected (ideally within 2 weeks of symptom onset). Early treatment shortens time to recovery and reduces ventilator requirement. Additionally, respiratory monitoring (FVC) should be proactive, not reactive.
- Observe since sensation intact: Preserved sensation does NOT mean GBS is mild. Motor involvement is the primary and life-threatening feature. This patient's FVC is already at 40% predicted — respiratory failure is imminent. GBS characteristically preserves sensation while causing devastating motor weakness. The areflexia alone demands treatment.
- Cyclophosphamide: Cyclophosphamide is used for severe autoimmune conditions (vasculitis, lupus nephritis) but has no role in GBS. GBS is an acute monophasic illness that responds to IVIG or plasmapheresis. Immunosuppressants like cyclophosphamide are used for CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy), the chronic counterpart of GBS.

Reference: GBS - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532254/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with 3 months of fluctuating bilateral ptosis worse at the end of the day, diplopia with sustained gaze, and progressive difficulty swallowing. Symptoms are BETTER in the morning and WORSEN with activity. Ice pack test: ptosis improves after applying ice to the eyelid for 2 minutes.LabsAChR binding antibodies Positive (elevated)CT chest 3.2 cm anterior mediastinal mass (thymoma)ExamEyes Bilateral ptosis, worsens with sustained upgaze (fatiguability)Facial Weak eye closure, transverse smile ("myasthenic snarl")Bulbar Nasal speech, weak palateLimbs Proximal > distal weakness, 4/5Reflexes NormalSensation Normal What is the treatment plan, and what life-threatening complication must be anticipated?

choices.text: Steroids alone are sufficient for management
Myasthenia gravis with thymoma — multi-modal treatment:
Increase pyridostigmine dose until respiratory symptoms resolve
No thymectomy needed since medications control symptoms

explanation:
Correct Answer: Myasthenia gravis: autoimmune antibodies against nicotinic ACh receptors at the NMJ → fatigable weakness. Classic features: fluctuating weakness that worsens with activity and improves with rest. Ocular (ptosis, diplopia) in 85%, generalized (bulbar, limb, respiratory) in 80%. Ice pack test: cold inhibits acetylcholinesterase → more ACh available → temporary improvement (sensitivity 80%). Treatment layers: (1) Symptomatic: pyridostigmine (AChE inhibitor). (2) Immunosuppression: steroids + steroid-sparing (azathioprine, mycophenolate). (3) Thymectomy: for thymoma (ALL patients) and non-thymoma generalized MG age 18-65 (MGTX trial: better outcomes at 3 years). Myasthenic crisis (15-20% of MG patients): respiratory failure requiring intubation. Triggers: infection, surgery, medication changes, aminoglycosides. Treatment: IVIG or plasmapheresis. Cholinergic crisis: pyridostigmine overdose → excessive ACh → SLUDGE (salivation, lacrimation, urination, diarrhea, GI distress, emesis) + weakness.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Fluctuating weakness worse with activity, better with rest = NMJ fatigability
- Bilateral ptosis + diplopia + dysphagia = generalized MG (ocular + bulbar)
- AChR antibodies positive = seropositive MG (85% of generalized MG)
- Thymoma on CT = mandatory thymectomy (10-15% of MG patients have thymoma)
- Bulbar weakness (dysphagia, nasal speech) = risk for aspiration and myasthenic crisis
- Pyridostigmine for symptom control + immunosuppression + thymectomy = standard triple approach

Evidence: Myasthenia Gravis - StatPearls - Gilhus NE, et al. StatPearls. NBK559331. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Steroids alone: While steroids are an important component of MG treatment, they are insufficient as monotherapy. This patient has a THYMOMA requiring surgical removal (thymectomy), needs pyridostigmine for symptomatic relief, and will need a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant for long-term management. Additionally, steroids can cause transient WORSENING of MG in the first 2 weeks of initiation.
- Thymectomy alone: Thymectomy addresses the immunologic source but does not provide immediate symptom control. Patients need pyridostigmine for daily symptom management and immunosuppression to control the autoimmune process. After thymectomy, many patients still require medications (though often at lower doses). Only 30-40% achieve complete remission after thymectomy.
- Keep increasing pyridostigmine: This is DANGEROUS. Excessive pyridostigmine causes CHOLINERGIC CRISIS: paradoxically increased weakness (depolarization block at NMJ) PLUS muscarinic symptoms (SLUDGE: salivation, lacrimation, urination, diarrhea, GI distress, emesis). Cholinergic crisis mimics myasthenic crisis but the treatment is opposite (stop pyridostigmine, not increase it). In respiratory failure, IVIG or plasmapheresis is the treatment.
- No thymectomy needed: Thymectomy is MANDATORY when a thymoma is present — thymomas are neoplasms with malignant potential (can invade locally and rarely metastasize). Even without thymoma, the MGTX trial showed thymectomy improves outcomes in generalized MG age 18-65. This patient has both a thymoma AND generalized MG — thymectomy is clearly indicated.

Reference: Myasthenia Gravis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559331/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with 4 days of progressive LEFT eye pain (worse with eye movement) and blurred vision. She had an episode of numbness and tingling in both legs 8 months ago that resolved spontaneously over 6 weeks. On exam, visual acuity is 20/100 OS (left) and 20/20 OD (right). She has a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD, Marcus Gunn pupil) in the left eye and pain with extraocular movements.ExamVisual 20/100 OS, 20/20 ODRAPD Present left eye (Marcus Gunn)Color vision Desaturated red left eyeFundoscopy Mild disc pallor left, normal rightMRI brain Multiple periventricular and juxtacortical T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions with one gadolinium-enhancing lesionMRI spine 2 T2 hyperintense lesions in cervical cordOCBs Positive in CSF (not matched in serum) How does this establish the diagnosis, and what is the acute and long-term treatment?

choices.text: Treat the optic neuritis and wait for more episodes before diagnosing MS
Multiple sclerosis (McDonald criteria 2017) — diagnosis established by demonstrating dissemination in SPACE (lesions in ≥2 CNS
Oral prednisone is equivalent to IV methylprednisolone for acute relapses
No disease-modifying therapy is needed until the third relapse
The optic neuritis is unrelated to the previous leg symptoms

explanation:
Correct Answer: MS diagnosis: McDonald criteria 2017 require dissemination in SPACE (DIS) and TIME (DIT). DIS: ≥1 T2 lesion in ≥2 of 4 characteristic locations (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord). DIT: simultaneous gadolinium-enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on a single MRI, OR new T2/enhancing lesion on follow-up MRI, OR a second clinical attack. This patient: DIS met (periventricular + juxtacortical + spinal cord = 3 locations), DIT met (current enhancing lesion + prior clinical episode 8 months ago). Optic neuritis: presenting symptom of MS in 20%; 50% of optic neuritis patients develop MS within 15 years. RAPD (Marcus Gunn pupil): affected eye has reduced direct response but intact consensual = afferent defect from optic nerve damage. Treatment paradigm shift: early high-efficacy DMT reduces long-term disability (treat early, treat aggressively). IV steroids for acute relapses ONLY speed recovery; they do NOT change long-term outcomes.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Optic neuritis (eye pain with movement, visual loss, RAPD) = classic MS presentation
- Prior leg numbness 8 months ago = separate clinical attack = DIT established
- MRI: periventricular + juxtacortical + spinal cord = DIS (3 of 4 locations)
- Gadolinium-enhancing lesion = active inflammation
- CSF OCBs positive = supports MS diagnosis (present in 95%)
- 28-year-old woman = peak MS demographic
- Two clinical attacks + MRI evidence = MS definitively diagnosed per McDonald 2017

Evidence: Multiple Sclerosis - StatPearls - Thompson AJ, et al. StatPearls. NBK499849. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Wait for more episodes: The 2017 McDonald criteria allow diagnosis on the FIRST clinical presentation if MRI demonstrates DIS and DIT. This patient meets both criteria. Delaying diagnosis delays disease-modifying therapy, and every relapse causes cumulative axonal damage. The era of "wait and see" has been replaced by "diagnose early, treat early."
- Oral prednisone equivalent to IV: The ONTT (Optic Neuritis Treatment Trial) showed that oral prednisone at standard doses was NOT equivalent to IV methylprednisolone and was actually associated with HIGHER relapse rates. IV methylprednisolone 1g/day x 3-5 days is the standard for acute MS relapses. Some recent data suggest high-dose oral methylprednisolone (1250mg) may be equivalent, but standard oral prednisone is not.
- Wait until third relapse for DMT: Modern MS treatment emphasizes EARLY initiation of DMT. Each relapse causes axonal damage that accumulates over time. The BENEFIT trial and others showed that early treatment significantly reduces long-term disability compared to delayed treatment. Waiting for a third relapse allows preventable neurologic damage.
- Optic neuritis unrelated to leg symptoms: The combination of optic neuritis NOW plus leg numbness 8 MONTHS AGO in a young woman with characteristic MRI lesions is textbook MS. These episodes represent attacks in DIFFERENT CNS locations (optic nerve and spinal cord) at DIFFERENT TIMES — the very definition of dissemination in space and time. Dismissing the connection misses the diagnosis.

Reference: Multiple Sclerosis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499849/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: An 82-year-old man with Parkinson disease, diabetes, and hypertension fell after getting up from his chair. He felt lightheaded for a few seconds then lost consciousness briefly. He takes lisinopril, amlodipine, tamsulosin, and carbidopa-levodopa. Standing vital signs show a significant blood pressure drop.Vital SignsBP supine 148/82BP standing (3 min) 108/62 (drop 40/20)HR supine 72HR standing 78 (minimal compensatory increase)ExamNeuro Mild resting tremor, bradykinesia (Parkinson features)Cardiac Regular rhythm, no murmur What confirms the diagnosis, and what factors are contributing?

choices.text: ORTHOSTATIC HYPOTENSION — defined as SBP drop ≥20 mmHg OR DBP drop ≥10

explanation:
Correct Answer: PIVOT: Orthostatic hypotension

Diagnostic criteria:
• SBP drop ≥20 mmHg, OR
• DBP drop ≥10 mmHg, OR
• Symptoms with standing + BP drop

Causes:
• Medications (antihypertensives, alpha-blockers, diuretics)
• Autonomic dysfunction (Parkinson, diabetes, MSA)
• Volume depletion
• Adrenal insufficiency
• Age-related baroreceptor decline

Key clue: Inadequate HR increase with standing suggests autonomic failure. Normal response: HR increases 10-20 bpm to compensate for BP drop.

Distinguishing from vasovagal:
• Orthostatic: occurs immediately upon standing
• Vasovagal: occurs after prolonged standing, has prodrome, trigger

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Syncope immediately upon standing (not prolonged standing like vasovagal)
- BP drop 40/20 mmHg = far exceeds diagnostic threshold (20/10)
- HR only increased 6 bpm = autonomic failure (inadequate compensation)
- Parkinson disease = autonomic dysfunction common
- Diabetes = autonomic neuropathy
- Three antihypertensives = polypharmacy contributing
- Tamsulosin (alpha-blocker) = particularly associated with orthostatic hypotension

Evidence: Orthostatic Hypotension - StatPearls - Freeman R, et al. StatPearls. NBK532938. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Cardiac arrhythmia: Arrhythmic syncope is typically sudden without positional component. This patient's syncope was clearly related to standing, and orthostatic vitals confirm a 40/20 mmHg BP drop. The normal ECG and regular rhythm make arrhythmia unlikely as the cause.
- Vasovagal syncope: Vasovagal occurs after PROLONGED standing (minutes) and has a prodrome (nausea, warmth, diaphoresis). This patient had syncope IMMEDIATELY upon standing with a documented >20/10 mmHg BP drop. The mechanism is orthostatic hypotension, not vasovagal reflex.
- Carotid sinus hypersensitivity: Carotid sinus hypersensitivity causes syncope with carotid sinus stimulation (tight collar, head turning, shaving). It would be diagnosed with carotid sinus massage. This patient's syncope was positional (standing) with documented orthostatic vital sign changes.
- Seizure: Parkinson disease is not associated with seizures. Seizures cause post-ictal confusion lasting minutes to hours, and are not related to position changes. This patient had brief LOC upon standing with immediate recovery — classic orthostatic syncope.

Reference: Orthostatic Hypotension - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532938/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 58-year-old man presents with tremor in both hands that has gradually worsened over 10 years. The tremor is most noticeable when he holds a cup of coffee or writes. It improves after a glass of wine. His father had a similar tremor. He has no difficulty with balance, walking, or buttoning his shirt.ExamHands Bilateral symmetric tremor, 6-8 Hz, postural (arms extended) and kinetic (finger-to-nose); NO tremor at restTone Normal (no rigidity)Gait Normal, arm swing preservedFace Normal expression, no hypomimiaWriting Large, tremulous letters What distinguishes this from Parkinson disease?

choices.text: Early Parkinson disease that hasn't developed other features yet
ESSENTIAL TREMOR (ET) — the most common movement disorder;

explanation:
Correct Answer: PIVOT: Essential tremor vs Parkinson

| Feature | Essential Tremor | Parkinson |
|---------|-----------------|----------|
| Onset | Bilateral, symmetric | UNILATERAL |
| Type | Action/postural | RESTING ('pill-rolling') |
| Frequency | 6-12 Hz (fast) | 4-6 Hz (slow) |
| Rigidity | Absent | Cogwheel |
| Bradykinesia | Absent | Present |
| Gait | Normal | Shuffling, reduced arm swing |
| Alcohol | IMPROVES | No effect |
| Writing | Large, tremulous | Small (micrographia) |
| Family history | Often positive (AD) | Usually sporadic |
| Treatment | Propranolol/primidone | Carbidopa-levodopa |

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 10-year gradual bilateral tremor = ET timeline
- Both hands affected symmetrically = ET (Parkinson starts unilateral)
- Postural + kinetic tremor = action tremor (ET pattern)
- NO resting tremor = argues against Parkinson
- Improves with alcohol = classic ET feature
- Father had similar tremor = autosomal dominant pattern
- Normal tone, no rigidity = no Parkinson features
- Normal gait with arm swing = no Parkinson gait
- Large tremulous writing = ET (vs micrographia in Parkinson)

Evidence: Essential Tremor - StatPearls - Louis ED, et al. StatPearls. NBK499261. 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Early Parkinson: Parkinson disease starts UNILATERALLY with RESTING tremor, and always develops other cardinal features (rigidity, bradykinesia, postural instability). This patient has had BILATERAL symmetric ACTION tremor for 10 years with NO rigidity, bradykinesia, or gait changes — if this were Parkinson, additional features would have appeared by now.
- Enhanced physiologic tremor: Physiologic tremor is low-amplitude, high-frequency (8-12 Hz), and present in everyone. Enhanced physiologic tremor is exaggerated by anxiety, caffeine, hyperthyroidism, or medications. It does NOT worsen over 10 years, does NOT have family history, and resolves when the precipitant is removed.
- Cerebellar tremor: Cerebellar tremor is an INTENTION tremor (worsens as approaching target) with dysmetria, not a postural tremor. It is typically UNILATERAL and associated with other cerebellar signs (ataxia, nystagmus, dysarthria). This patient's bilateral postural tremor with family history is classic ET.
- Wilson disease: Wilson disease should be considered in patients <40 with tremor + psychiatric symptoms + liver disease. This 58-year-old with 10 years of pure bilateral tremor, family history, and no psychiatric/hepatic features has essential tremor, not Wilson disease.

Reference: Essential Tremor - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK499261/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 55-year-old man with epilepsy is brought to the ED with continuous generalized tonic-clonic seizure activity lasting 10 minutes. He is cyanotic and has not regained consciousness. His wife reports he ran out of his levetiracetam 4 days ago. IV access has been established.Vital SignsHR 130SpO2 84%Temp 38.8°C What is the treatment algorithm, and what is the time threshold that defines this condition?

choices.text: Observation — most seizures stop within 15 minutes without treatment
Status epilepticus (seizure ≥5 min or ≥2 without return to baseline) —
Phenytoin first-line, benzodiazepines are too sedating
Intubate before any medication — secure airway is always first
CT scan of head before treatment to rule out structural cause

explanation:
Correct Answer: Status epilepticus: seizure ≥5 minutes or ≥2 seizures without return to baseline. TREATMENT ALGORITHM (time-based): (0-5 min) ABCs, IV access, glucose, thiamine → (5-20 min) FIRST-LINE: IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg (max 4mg, may repeat x1) OR IV diazepam OR IM midazolam (if no IV) → (20-40 min) SECOND-LINE: IV fosphenytoin 20 PE/kg (or levetiracetam 60mg/kg or valproate 40mg/kg) → (40+ min) REFRACTORY: intubation + continuous IV infusion (propofol, midazolam, or pentobarbital) with continuous EEG monitoring. Lorazepam preferred over diazepam (longer anticonvulsant effect in brain despite shorter half-life). Causes: medication non-compliance (most common), stroke, infection, metabolic, trauma.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- 10 minutes of continuous seizure = status epilepticus (≥5 min)
- Levetiracetam non-compliance = most common cause
- IV lorazepam = first-line (already has IV access)
- If no IV: IM midazolam (RAMPART trial — equally effective)
- Benzodiazepine NOW — do not delay for imaging
- If benzo fails (20 min): load fosphenytoin or levetiracetam IV
- Refractory (40+ min): intubation + continuous infusion

Evidence: Status Epilepticus - StatPearls - Brophy GM, et al. Neurocrit Care. 2012;17:3-23.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Observation: Seizures lasting >5 minutes are unlikely to stop spontaneously and risk progressive neuronal damage. Status epilepticus has increasing mortality with each minute untreated. Immediate benzodiazepine is standard of care.
- Phenytoin first-line: Phenytoin/fosphenytoin is SECOND-LINE, given only after benzodiazepine failure. Benzodiazepines act faster (onset <2 min for IV lorazepam vs 20-30 min for phenytoin) and are more effective as initial treatment.
- Intubate first: While airway management is important, intubation should NOT delay benzodiazepine administration. Stopping the seizure with lorazepam often improves oxygenation. BVM ventilation and positioning can support the airway while benzodiazepine is given.
- CT before treatment: Imaging should NEVER delay treatment of status epilepticus. CT is obtained AFTER seizure control to evaluate for structural causes. Treatment is always empiric and immediate.

Reference: Status Epilepticus - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430765/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 42-year-old man with epilepsy presents with generalized tonic-clonic seizure activity lasting 8 minutes. He received lorazepam 4 mg IV 5 minutes ago without cessation of seizure activity. IV access is established.Vital SignsBP 170/100HR 120RR 28Temp 100.2°FSpO₂ 94% Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: Stat CT head before further treatment

explanation:
Correct Answer: When benzodiazepines fail to terminate status epilepticus, the next step is a second-line antiseizure medication: fosphenytoin (20 mg PE/kg IV), levetiracetam, or valproate. Do not delay treatment for imaging.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Status epilepticus: seizure >5 min or recurrent without return to baseline
- First-line: benzodiazepine (lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg). If fails after 5-10 min → second-line
- Second-line: fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg, levetiracetam 60 mg/kg, or valproate 40 mg/kg
- ESETT trial showed equivalent efficacy of all three second-line agents
- Continuous infusion (midazolam, propofol) is third-line for refractory status

Evidence: Status Epilepticus - StatPearls - Betjemann JP, Bhatt A. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK430686. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Repeat lorazepam: Repeated benzodiazepines have diminishing returns due to GABA receptor internalization; move to second-line.
- Intubation: May be needed but should not delay second-line AED administration.
- Continuous midazolam: Third-line for refractory SE; premature before trying second-line agents.
- CT head first: Treatment must not be delayed for imaging in active status epilepticus.

Reference: Status Epilepticus - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430686/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 58-year-old woman with known myasthenia gravis presents with worsening dysphagia, nasal speech, and dyspnea over 24 hours. She developed symptoms after starting azithromycin for pneumonia 3 days ago. FVC is 800 mL (predicted 2,800 mL).Vital SignsBP 148/88HR 100RR 28Temp 99.8°FSpO₂ 92% Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate next step?

choices.text: Elective intubation, discontinue azithromycin, and start IVIG or plasmapheresis
Non-invasive positive pressure ventilation and observation

explanation:
Correct Answer: Myasthenic crisis with FVC elective intubation, removal of the offending agent (azithromycin), and rapid immunotherapy (IVIG or plasmapheresis). Do not wait for respiratory failure.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- FVC <1 L or <15 mL/kg = impending respiratory failure → elective intubation
- Macrolides (azithromycin), aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones exacerbate MG
- IVIG and plasmapheresis work within days; both have equivalent efficacy
- High-dose steroids can transiently worsen MG ('steroid dip') — avoid in crisis
- Increasing pyridostigmine in crisis risks cholinergic crisis

Evidence: Myasthenic Crisis - StatPearls - Wendell LC, Levine JM. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK482272. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- Increase pyridostigmine: Risk of cholinergic crisis; will not address immunologic cause of exacerbation.
- High-dose prednisone: Can cause 'steroid dip' — transient worsening in first 2 weeks — dangerous in crisis.
- Edrophonium test: Diagnostic test, not treatment; risky in patients with respiratory compromise.
- NIPPV and observe: FVC <1 L warrants intubation; NIPPV may delay necessary airway protection.

Reference: Myasthenic Crisis - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482272/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 28-year-old woman with known migraines presents with a severe unilateral throbbing headache with photophobia and nausea for 6 hours. She took ibuprofen 800 mg at onset without relief. She has no focal neurologic deficits and no fever.Vital SignsBP 128/78HR 88RR 16Temp 98.4°FSpO₂ 99% Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in acute management?
explanation:
Correct Answer: For acute migraine refractory to NSAIDs, triptans (sumatriptan SC, oral, or nasal) are first-line rescue therapy. Subcutaneous sumatriptan has the fastest onset (10-15 min) and highest efficacy.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- Triptans are 5-HT1B/1D agonists — cause cerebral vasoconstriction and inhibit trigeminal nociception
- SC sumatriptan has fastest onset; oral and nasal are alternatives
- Contraindicated in hemiplegic migraine, uncontrolled HTN, coronary artery disease
- Dexamethasone is adjunctive (reduces recurrence) but not primary abortive therapy

Evidence: Migraine - StatPearls - Pescador Ruschel MA, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK560787. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- CT head: No red flags present; imaging not indicated for typical migraine without concerning features.
- IV dexamethasone alone: Reduces migraine recurrence but is adjunctive, not primary abortive treatment.
- Lumbar puncture: Not indicated without thunderclap onset, meningeal signs, or fever.
- Start topiramate: Prophylactic medication; does not address acute attack.

Reference: Migraine - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK560787/ [Accessed 2025]

stem: A 35-year-old man presents with 5 days of progressive ascending symmetric weakness starting in his feet, now to his thighs. He had gastroenteritis 2 weeks ago. Reflexes are absent in lower extremities. FVC is 1.8 L (predicted 4.5 L — 40%).Vital SignsBP 140/85HR 95RR 22Temp 98.6°FSpO₂ 97% Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.text: ICU admission, serial FVC monitoring, and IVIG or plasmapheresis
Nerve conduction study before starting treatment

explanation:
Correct Answer: GBS with FVC ICU admission with serial FVC monitoring and immunotherapy (IVIG or plasmapheresis). Do not delay treatment for NCS. Steroids are NOT effective in GBS.

Clinical reasoning pitfalls:
- GBS: acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy, typically post-infectious
- FVC 30% decline)
- IVIG (0.4 g/kg x 5 days) and plasmapheresis are equally effective
- Corticosteroids are NOT effective and may worsen outcomes
- NCS confirms diagnosis but should not delay treatment when clinical picture is classic

Evidence: Guillain-Barré Syndrome - StatPearls - Willison HJ, et al. StatPearls [Internet]. NBK532264. Updated 2024.

Why other answers are incorrect:
- IV methylprednisolone: Steroids are not effective in GBS and may worsen outcomes.
- Oral prednisone: Steroids have no role in GBS treatment.
- NCS before treatment: Should not delay treatment when clinical presentation is classic.
- Observation on floor: FVC at 40% predicted requires ICU-level monitoring; respiratory failure risk is high.

Reference: Guillain-Barré Syndrome - StatPearls - https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK532264/ [Accessed 2025]
